articles.title: On the Radar: Brazil’s ‘Escobar,’ Haiti’s Latest Massacre, and Venezuela’s Former First Lady Under Scrutiny
articles.description: This week’s On the Radar highlights Haiti’s latest massacre, Brazil’s “Pablo Escobar,” and the legal troubles of Venezuela’s former first lady.

articles.title: The Many Lives of ‘Iván Mordisco,’ Colombia’s Most Wanted
articles.description: President Gustavo Petro has intensified operations against Iván Mordisco, Colombia’s most wanted criminal. But, after years of military offensives and false reports of his death, the guerrilla leader continues to elude authorities.

articles.title: From Airstrikes to Cooperation: Will the ‘New Phase’ of Ecuador’s Drug War Deliver?
articles.description: Ecuador’s revamping of old security measures like curfews and mass arrests of suspected gang collaborators are now being complemented with airstrikes backed by the United States and operations against transatlantic drug trafficking organizations in tandem with European agencies. But will this

articles.title: The Corruption Engine Behind Sebastián Marset’s Empire
articles.description: A Paraguayan senator was convicted for laundering cocaine profits through real estate and a soccer club, and supporting trafficking via his private plane — exposing how trafficker Sebastián Marset’s alleged network infiltrated politics and business across the region.

articles.title: On the Radar: Cocaine Power Shifts and Seizures
articles.description: Allegations link Colombia’s President Gustavo Petro to drug trafficking, while Venezuela’s regime, and its Cartel of the Suns, process a major personnel shift. In Chile, a major cocaine and ketamine seizure raises eyebrows.

articles.title: Map: Military Strikes Target Complex Criminal Landscape on Colombia-Ecuador Border
articles.description: InSight Crime maps the complex criminal dynamics at play in the Colombia-Ecuador border region, one of the world’s most important cocaine transit corridors.

articles.title: Carnival, Samba, and Criminal Control in Rio de Janeiro
articles.description: In Rio de Janeiro, the family clans that control informal lotteries in neighborhoods around the city have latched onto samba schools at the heart of Brazil’s Carnival to launder their image and bolster their political and social power.

articles.title: How Peru’s Chronic Political Instability Enables Organized Crime
articles.description: Peru’s chronic inability to form a stable government has left the country’s institutions feckless in the face of an alarming expansion of organized crime groups.

articles.title: On the Radar: Latin America Keeps Attacking Crime — But Is It Working?
articles.description: Bombings on Ecuador’s border with Colombia, the arrest of one of the region’s most wanted traffickers, and more hardline anti-crime measures from Chile are the focus this week. But is any of it working?

articles.title: What Does the Exit of Vladimir Padrino López Mean for Organized Crime in Venezuela?
articles.description: What does the dismissal of Vladimir Padrino López mean for criminal dynamics in Venezuela and the future of the Cartel of the Suns?

articles.title: Dollarino and the Rise of Brokers in the Cocaine Pipeline to Europe
articles.description: The upcoming trial of Emanuele Gregorini in Italy offers an inside look at the growing role that brokers play in connecting drug trafficking networks in Europe and Latin America.

articles.title: 5 Models of Criminal Control Over Fentanyl Markets in Northern Mexico
articles.description: In various parts of Mexico, local drug markets do not operate according to supply-and-demand logics alone. What is sold, how it is sold, how much it costs, where it is […]

articles.title: How Fentanyl Was Introduced in Mexico’s Local Drug Markets
articles.description: As Mexican criminal organizations became the main suppliers of illicit fentanyl to the United States, small pockets of domestic consumption began to emerge along key trafficking corridors. Since at least […]

articles.title: How Criminal Decisions Over the Drug Market Impact Public Health in Northern Mexico
articles.description: As international drug policy increasingly moves toward public health strategies, the experiences in northern Mexico suggest that criminal control over local markets, and the decisions criminal actors make about supply, can severely challenge these efforts.

articles.title: What the Story of Meth Tells Us About the Future of Mexico’s CJNG
articles.description: The CJNG and El Mencho rose through Mexico’s criminal ranks alongside the explosion of methamphetamine production in the country.

articles.title: What Marset’s Capture Tells Us About the Cocaine Business
articles.description: The capture of Sebastián Marset, one of South America’s most wanted individuals, exemplifies why the takedown of major leaders should be just one part of a multi-pronged approach to tackling the drug trade.

articles.title: The Rise and Fall of Sebastián Marset
articles.description: Sebastián Marset was one of South America’s most wanted drug traffickers prior to his arrest in Bolivia. Learn more about his criminal career.

articles.title: On the Radar: What’s New in Trump’s Push for More Military Might to Fight Organized Crime?
articles.description: The founder of one of Mexico’s most powerful criminal groups pleads guilty, US President Trump’s new regional anti-drug push aims to up military cooperation against organized crime in the region, and nearly 50 people are injured in a nightclub grenade attack in Peru.

articles.title: 5 Things to Know About the Capture of Sebastián Marset
articles.description: Sebastián Marset was one of South America’s most wanted drug traffickers before his recent arrest in Bolivia, but how did the Uruguayan criminal leader evade capture for so long?

articles.title: Sebastián Marset
articles.description: Sebastián Marset is Uruguay’s most famous drug trafficker and the leader of an international network known as the First Uruguayan Cartel (Primer Cartel Uruguayo – PCU).

articles.title: Police Crisis Tests Security Strategy in Rosario, Argentina’s Organized Crime Hub
articles.description: The government’s security model for Rosario, Argentina’s most violent city, was seemingly showing results and bringing down homicide rates, but local police started to burn out.

articles.title: InSight Crime’s 2025 Homicide Round-Up
articles.description: Despite the widespread use of states of emergency, the increasingly militarized response to organized crime, and the growth of criminal economies, the murder rate in 2025 across Latin America and the Caribbean declined.

articles.title: Amid US Strikes, Booming Guyana Could Become Drug Trafficking Hub
articles.description: US military intervention in and near Venezuela could be displacing cocaine trafficking to other parts of the region, notably Guyana, whose new seaports and rapid economic growth increase its attractiveness for drug traffickers and organized crime.

articles.title: Breaking Analysis: LatAm Militaries Are Already Fighting ‘Cartels,’ But Trump Wants More
articles.description: US President Donald Trump says the only way to defeat organized crime, drug trafficking, and transnational gangs is by using military force.

articles.title: A CJNG Death in Mexico and A Missed Opportunity
articles.description: As the mainstream media dedicated its resources to understanding what was happening on the ground, it called on our in-depth understanding of the CJNG, El Mencho’s criminal career, and Mexico’s role in the criminal ecosystem to help the world make sense of what El Mencho’s death really meant.

articles.title: On the Radar: Ecuador’s Escalating Crime Crisis
articles.description: El Mencho’s lavish funeral in Mexico, why violence is rising in Ecuador despite hard-line security measures, and the future of the South American country’s fight against organized crime. are the focus of this week’s roundup of the main organized crime stories in Latin America.

articles.title: More Than Narco Wives: Women’s Hidden Power in Latin American Organized Crime
articles.description: Women continue to be a fundamental part of Latin America’s organized crime groups and have held high-ranking positions in the drug business.

articles.title: What Does El Mencho’s Killing in Mexico Mean for LatAm Cocaine Flows?
articles.description: A patchwork of strategic alliances means the death of the CJNG’s former leader El Mencho is unlikely to disrupt the flow of cocaine to Central America and the United States.

articles.title: Is Brazil’s Biggest Port Winning the Fight Against Drug Traffickers?
articles.description: Authorities at Brazil’s Port of Santos are analyzing new intelligence and data to identify how drug traffickers operate and intercept them in the act.

articles.title: Breaking Analysis: What Does the Killing of ‘El Mencho’ Mean for Mexico’s Drug War?
articles.description: How is the killing of CJNG leader Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias “El Mencho” going to impact the organization he led, as well as Mexico’s criminal future? InSight Crime Investigator Victoria Dittmar and Co-Director Steven Dudley discuss, drawing on lessons from the past.

articles.title: On the Radar: El Mencho’s Capture – Gamechanger or Business as Usual?
articles.description: The death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias “El Mencho,” leader of the Jalisco Cartel New Generation, shook Mexico’s criminal landscape. In On the Radar, we analyze the impact of his fall and the different scenarios facing the Jalisco Cartel New Generation.

articles.title: How CJNG Control Works in Guadalajara, With or Without El Mencho
articles.description: Guadalajara’s criminal history predates the CJNG. Since at least the 1980s, the city has lived a double life, and today there is a high-functioning system of criminal control there which will continue to function despite the death of El Mencho.

articles.title: The Future of Drug Trafficking Out of Venezuela
articles.description: Drug trafficking out of Venezuela predates Nicolás Maduro and will long outlive him. The question is whether the cocaine trade will continue to be managed and regulated by the Chavista regime, or whether Maduro’s removal will weaken state-embedded drug trafficking.

articles.title: Camaraderie and Drug Trafficking: Colombian Guerrillas in Venezuela
articles.description: The ELN, in cooperation with Venezuelan authorities, has used several tactics to consolidate its control along the Colombia-Venezuela border. This binational boundary is crossed by countless drug trafficking corridors, an illegal economy that has financed both high-ranking regime officials and Colom

articles.title: The Evolution of the Cartel of the Suns
articles.description: The  Cartel of the Suns, while perhaps debilitated after Maduro’s capture, is still very much alive. What’s more, after numerous transformations over many decades, it looks set to undergo another transformation.

articles.title: The Criminal Portfolio Sustaining Military Loyalty in Venezuela
articles.description: For many members of Venezuela’s armed forces, drug trafficking, illegal mining and other criminal rents have become a parallel source of income that offset meager wages.

articles.title: The Shifting US Portrait of the Cartel of the Suns
articles.description: The United States justified the Maduro arrest as a law enforcement action intended to take down the leader of a powerful criminal organization. But US officials have offered evolving and sometimes contradictory descriptions of the so-called cartel that Maduro allegedly headed.

articles.title: The King Is Dead, Long Live the Queen
articles.description: The state-embedded drug trafficking structure in Venezuela is largely intact, although the conditions under which it operates have shifted dramatically.

articles.title: 5 CJNG Hotspots to Watch After El Mencho’s Killing in Mexico
articles.description: We outline five CJNG hotspots to keep an eye on in Mexico following the death of the group’s leader.

articles.title: CJNG Violence After the Killing of ‘El Mencho’: Power Play or Last Gasp?
articles.description: The government’s killing of “El Mencho” on February 22 provoked a spasm of violence by his cartel that has been, in some ways, unprecedented. But previous displays of force by aggrieved criminal groups have proven to be largely temporary and, in most instances, a last gasp before sputtering and atom

articles.title: What’s Next for Mexico’s CJNG After the Killing of ‘El Mencho’?
articles.description: Following El Mencho’s death, the CJNG faces a new era of succession and potential fragmentation. Who will lead the cartel and its criminal operations in Mexico now?

articles.title: Jalisco Cartel New Generation (CJNG)
articles.description: The Jalisco Cartel New Generation is often considered Mexico’s most feared cartel, whose operations stretch across the country.

articles.title: Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias ‘El Mencho’
articles.description: Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias “El Mencho,” has propelled the Jalisco Cartel’s growth to become a fierce and powerful criminal network.

articles.title: US Sanctions in Latin America Have Increased. But Can They Be Enforced?
articles.description: A wave of sanctions on drug traffickers and their associates has been compromised by a lag in enforcement, limiting the impact on transnational criminal networks.

articles.title: On the Radar: Football, Cocaine, and Corruption
articles.description: How football fuels Paraguay’s drug trade, the latest extradition request from Mexico to the US, and what the latest cocaine seizure in El Salvador reveals are the focus of this week’s roundup of the main organized crime stories in Latin America.

articles.title: How Guatemala’s Gang Crisis Reached a Boiling Point
articles.description:
Guatemala is returning to hardline security policies amid a resurgence of gang activity, two years after its president pledged a renewed approach to the problem. 
 But in attempting to target gang leadership, the government has exposed its limited capacity to do so.

articles.title: The State of Play: US Anti-Drug Cooperation in Latin America
articles.description: Regional cooperation with the United States on anti-narcotics is a central focus of US policy in Latin America. This interactive map shows the current role of each country in drug trafficking and the status of their anti-narcotics cooperation with the United States.

articles.title: On the Radar: Power, Corruption, and Criminality
articles.description: This week, we’re asking what the future might hold for the United States’ relationship with Honduras, what a mass kidnapping in Mexico tells us about the country’s crime wars, and whether another high-level political arrest in Ecuador signals progress in the fight against corruptio

articles.title: Colombia’s EMC Holds ‘Total Peace’ Hostage in Cauca
articles.description: At the heart of the conflict in Cauca is control of territory peppered with illegal gold mines, coca and marijuana crops, and a bridge connecting Colombia to the Pacific – a key exit point for drugs heading to international markets.

articles.title: Video: Are ICE’s Roundups in the United States an Effective Anti-Crime Strategy?
articles.description: The Trump administration has used the threat of organized crime to justify roundups of suspected undocumented migrants. InSight Crime’s Managing Editor Deborah Bonello and Senior Investigator James Bargent discuss the findings of our recent investigation into immigration enforcement operations in Na

articles.title: Nicolás Maduro
articles.description: The former president of Venezuela, Nicolás Maduro, oversaw the consolidation of a hybrid state in which governance and criminality became deeply entwined, transforming the country into a regional hub for organized crime.

articles.title: On the Radar: Presidents Talk Organized Crime
articles.description: Presidents Trump and Petro meet at the White House, Brazil’s Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva warns of AI’s potential to be weaponized by organized crime for elections, and Costa Rica’s president-elect Laura Fernández promises to be tough on crime.

articles.title: Top Criminal Takedowns of 2025
articles.description:
From underground bunkers to press conferences and malls, law enforcement nabbed some major crime bosses in 2025.  
 Here’s our take on the top criminal takedowns from 2025.

articles.title: Will the US Send Alleged Fuel Smugglers to Mexico?
articles.description: Mexico’s extradition request for several American citizens accused of smuggling stolen oil exposes an imbalance in the US-Mexico bilateral security relationship.

articles.title: As Brazil’s PCC Gentrifies, Favela Residents Must Fend for Themselves
articles.description: When the First Capital Command (Primeiro Comando da Capital – PCC) took over the brightly-colored hillside favelas in São Paulo, Brazil’s most powerful gang also brought the criminal underworld under control. But the gang’s Robin Hood days are a thing of the past.

articles.title: On the Radar: Violence from Gangs and Governments Rules the Region
articles.description: Gang massacres in Mexico, more hardline measures against drug-related violence in Ecuador, and legal action from the victims of Trump’s missile strikes in the Caribbean dominated the conversations around organized crime in the Americas this week. Here’s our take.

articles.title: Winner of Colombia-Ecuador Trade War Could Be Organized Crime
articles.description: The fight against organized crime is now at the center of a trade war that has erupted between Colombia and Ecuador — one that will likely benefit only the region’s criminal actors.

articles.title: Costa Rica’s Leading Presidential Candidates Embrace Hardline Security
articles.description: There are 19 candidates in the running, and polls show one, Laura Fernández, as the clear frontrunner. All the leading contenders’ security proposals largely point toward hardline measures, including expanding the police force, strengthening port security, and imposing harsher penalties, underscorin

articles.title: FARC Dissidents in Venezuela
articles.description: Venezuela is a vital base of operations for the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC), and three of its seven blocs (fighting divisions) have a presence there. The country provides the guerrillas with key drug trafficking corridors, a place to escape pressure from Colombian security for

articles.title: Organized Crime and AI: 5 Topics We Are Monitoring in 2026
articles.description: As advances in artificial intelligence bring it to nearly all corners of life, both criminal organizations and law enforcement are adopting these new technologies to try and gain an edge. These are the five areas we are watching most closely in 2026.

articles.title: Will Colombian Guerrillas Lose Their Safe Haven in Post‑Maduro Venezuela?
articles.description: The combination of former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro’s exit from power and increasing US military pressure has disrupted the criminal ecosystem in Venezuela, putting strain on Colombian guerrilla groups that have long benefitted from their ties to Chavismo and which now may retreat in

articles.title: On the Radar: Mexico Responds to US Pressure
articles.description: Violent gang reprisals in Guatemala, a symbolic push for justice in Colombia, and mounting pressure on Mexico from the United States are in the spotlight this week. Dive into the latest developments shaping the region’s security landscape.

articles.title: Álex Saab
articles.description: Álex Nain Saab Moran is a Colombian businessman accused of being one of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro’s main frontmen. International authorities accuse Saab of taking part in a multimillion-dollar corruption […]

articles.title: How InSight Crime Tracked Down an Explosive Narco Video in Honduras
articles.description: On September 3, 2024, InSight Crime published a video showing Carlos Zelaya, the brother-in-law of Honduran President Xiomara Castro, in a room full of the country’s most powerful drug traffickers. This is the story of how we got that video.

articles.title: Cartel of the Suns
articles.description: The “Cartel of the Suns” (Cartel de los Soles) is the term used to describe the shadowy groups inside Venezuela’s military that are involved in a wide range of criminal activities, including gasoline smuggling, illegal mining and other corruption schemes, but primarily drug trafficking.

articles.title: On the Radar: The Ripples of Maduro’s Removal Continue
articles.description: This week, we analyze the ripples of recent US operations in the Caribbean and Venezuela across the region. That includes tensions between the US and Mexico, violence along the Colombia–Venezuela border, and the continuation of record cocaine seizures between Latin America and Europe.

articles.title: InSight Crime Highlights 10 Years of GameChangers Series
articles.description: To start the new year, we celebrated our tenth edition of our GameChanger series with a donor-exclusive event with some of our top experts, looking back over the last decade to understand what 2026 could mean for organized crime in the Americas.

articles.title: ELN in Venezuela
articles.description: The National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Nacional – ELN) is the last active insurgency in Colombia, and the main criminal actor in the Colombia-Venezuela border. From there, the group has expanded into Venezuelan territory.

articles.title: 5 Criminal Figures Who Helped Develop Venezuela’s System of Corruption
articles.description:
The arrest of former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and his wife on drug trafficking charges on January 3 brought into sharp focus the involvement of corrupt Venezuelan state elements in drug trafficking. 
Here are five figures who played a prominent role in the development of Venezuelan state

articles.title: Tareck El Aissami
articles.description: Tareck El Aissami was Venezuela’s Vice President of the Economy and the country’s Minister of Industries and National Production until March 20, 2023, when he resigned after an anti-corruption operation arrested multiple high-ranking officials in his circle.

articles.title: Samark López Bello
articles.description: Samark López Bello is a Venezuelan businessman and currently a fugitive from American justice facing charges of laundering drug money.

articles.title: National Liberation Army (ELN)
articles.description: The National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Nacional – ELN) is Colombia’s last true insurgency and one of Latin America’s most powerful criminal organizations.

articles.title: Report Puts International Banks, Honduras Elites at Center of Berta Cáceres Murder
articles.description: The murder of renowned Indigenous activist and environmental defender Berta Cáceres in Honduras in 2016 was an organized criminal operation funded by money from two international development banks that was diverted away from a controversial extractive project to pay for illegal activities and murder

articles.title: The Colectivos’ Role in the Struggle for Power in Post-Maduro Venezuela
articles.description:
Tension has flooded Caracas since the early hours of January 3, when then-President Nicolás Maduro and his wife were arrested and taken to prison in the United States. In response, armed men with their faces covered by balaclavas poured into the capital’s streets. 
 These men are members of colectiv

articles.title: On the Radar: Venezuela’s New Criminal Chapter
articles.description: Who is going to control the country’s criminal economies following the exit of Nicolás Maduro? You can find profiles of groups, including the ELN and colectivos, as well as players such as Diosdado Cabello, in our archive.

articles.title: 5 Crime Groups You Need to Know to Understand Post-Maduro Venezuela
articles.description:
The arrest of Nicolás Maduro marks a new chapter for organized crime in Venezuela. The country’s slew of criminal groups will have to adjust their operations and alliances amid a political shakeup that is certain to have impacts across the underworld.  
 These are five of the country’s most pr

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: US Reignites the War on Drugs and Weakens It for 2026
articles.description: The quantum shift in US security policy in Latin America and the Caribbean under President Donald Trump in 2025 has moved towards a militarized and repressive model and is forsaking the most successful elements of the fight against transnational organized crime: cooperation and intelligence sharing.

articles.title: How Maduro’s Ouster Will Shift Criminal Dynamics in Venezuela
articles.description: US President Donald Trump insisted that the capture of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro has removed the “kingpin of a vast criminal network,” but it will not upend the Venezuelan government’s permissive stance toward organized crime.

articles.title: 3 Criminal Consequences of Maduro’s Exit From Venezuela
articles.description: The dramatic arrest of Venezuela’s President Nicolás Maduro this weekend poses three key questions about the future of the criminal groups and dynamics in the South American country and Latin America.

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: Colombia’s Total Peace Remains in Pieces
articles.description: In 2025, Colombia faced levels of violence not seen in years, driven by clashes among criminal groups that have strengthened their military power and territorial control in areas critical to illegal economies.This historical backdrop is essential to understanding how Colombia’s security dynamics may

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: Cryptocurrency Is Now in the Criminal Mainstream
articles.description: A decade ago, Latin America’s criminal groups were starting to experiment with cryptocurrency, but in 2025, they went all in. And as the amount of cocaine trafficked around the globe exploded, rigid criminal organizations have been replaced by loose, modular networks that invest in innovation and te

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: The Unstoppable Rise of Environmental Crime
articles.description: In 2025, environmental crimes spread into corners of the Amazon Basin that had so far largely escaped the rampant plunder of the forest and its inhabitants by criminal networks and regional elites. Over the past decade, organized crime has played an increasingly central role in the climate crisis fa

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: The 10-Year Evolution of Latin America’s Booming Marijuana Market
articles.description: In a year where cocaine and fentanyl garnered international attention, marijuana continued to play a key role within the regional criminal landscape. During 2025, marijuana was again one of the most promising criminal markets in Latin America and the Caribbean.

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: Migration Smuggling Market Goes From Boom to Bust
articles.description: The number of migrants traveling through Latin America to the United States plummeted in 2025 and sent organized crime groups scrambling to fill a huge hole in their profit. The abrupt turnaround is the result of major policy changes in the United States, which have  have brought a crashing halt to

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: Top Criminal Newsmakers This Year
articles.description:
In a year that saw a dramatic reset to the fight against the drug trade and organized crime in the Americas by the administration of US President Donald Trump, the following Latin American criminal groups were rarely out of the headlines.  
 All of these groups have featured prominently in our cover

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: How the Trump Administration Reset the Fight Against Organized Crime
articles.description: From drug boat bombings to terrorist designations to WMDs, in 2025, the Trump administration upended the way the United States confronts criminals, but its impact on organized crime going forward is far from clear.

articles.title: How Organized Crime Threatens the Amazon’s Uncontacted Tribes
articles.description: Organized crime has become an existential threat to the uncontacted Indigenous peoples of the Amazon as illegal gold miners, drug traffickers, loggers, and poachers advance ever deeper into the world’s largest rainforest.

articles.title: What Happens Now That the US Has Labeled Fentanyl a WMD
articles.description: US President Donald Trump has designated the synthetic drug fentanyl as a weapon of mass destruction, putting the drug on par with nuclear bombs and weaponized biological agents, and further propelling the war on drugs to the forefront of his national security agenda.

articles.title: How Mexico’s Fuel Theft Market Went Transnational
articles.description: Mexico’s largest criminal groups are expanding the sophistication and profitability of their illicit fuel markets beyond local theft into cross-border smuggling networks that affect the US energy sector.

articles.title: InSight Crime Investigator Talks Organized Crime at UN Forum
articles.description: InSight Crime investigator Victoria Dittmar spoke at the Global Humanitarian Policy Forum on December 11 to discuss the humanitarian implications and risks that organized crime poses in the Americas, as well as the key dynamics to watch in 2026.

articles.title: On the Radar: Violence Spikes in Battles for Control in Haiti, Ecuador, and Mexico
articles.description: This week we look at surges of violence in Haiti, Ecuador and Mexico, where struggles for territorial and market supremacy by criminal groups is pushing governments to respond with force. But can they regain control?

articles.title: Corruption Looms Over Record Marijuana Seizure in Paraguay
articles.description: Forty police officers are under investigation after Paraguay’s anti-drug unit made a record-breaking marijuana seizure, in a case that underscores how endemic corruption has historically frustrated anti-trafficking operations and sowed distrust among authorities.

articles.title: What a ‘Historic’ Marijuana Seizure Says About Smuggling Dynamics in Argentina
articles.description: A recent seizure of nearly 10 tons of marijuana in Argentina was portrayed by the government as an historic blow to organized crime, but it actually highlighted a recurring smuggling dynamic on the South American country’s northern border.

articles.title: Border Wars: The ELN and the Takeover of Catatumbo
articles.description: The ELN offensive was also the last straw for the peace process with the government of President Gustavo Petro. Following the Catatumbo incident, the Colombian government suspended dialogue with the ELN, leaving a rift that is still unmended. And with presidential elections next year, it likely deal

articles.title: The Future of the ELN
articles.description: The future of the National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Nacional – ELN) is now intimately connected to Venezuela. As long as the Venezuelan government supports the Colombian rebel group, it cannot be fully defeated and is unlikely to sign any peace agreement.

articles.title: Guerrilla Governance: The ELN’s Social Control in Táchira
articles.description: The ELN’s impunity in Táchira is the result of years of complicity and cooperation with local security forces and pro-government leaders. In many areas, the ELN has gained such profound social control that citizens view the group as an extension of the state – perhaps even more reliable than Preside

articles.title: Mirror of Control: How the ELN Dominates Both Arauca and Apure
articles.description: It took decades for the ELN to achieve hegemony in Arauca. But more recently, it has solidified a similar level of social control on the other side of the border, in the Venezuelan state of Apure, where it operates with the complicity of not just local, but national state elements.

articles.title: Cocaine Corridor: Drugs Drive ELN’s Venezuela Expansion
articles.description: The ELN has now reportedly expanded to operating its own cocaine laboratories and overseeing transport of the drug across borders. The group’s seizure of Colombia’s coca-rich Catatumbo border region in early 2025 was a big step in a wider, planned expansion into the drug trade, likely in tandem with

articles.title: On the Radar: US Targets and Negotiates with Traffickers
articles.description: This week’s major developments show contradictions in US policy, with the Trump administration fighting the drug war with one hand and doing deals with traffickers with the other.

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Analyzes Pardon of Former Honduras President
articles.description: InSight Crime Co-director Steven Dudley recently discussed the pardon of former Honduras President Juan Orlando Hernández with Marco Werman, host of The World public radio program.

articles.title: Could Armed Groups Backed by Maduro Resist a US Invasion of Venezuela?
articles.description: With the United States weighing up a military intervention to remove Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro from power, the criminal organizations aligned with the regime could soon face a critical choice: protect their economic interests or stand by their political patrons.

articles.title: InSight Crime Managing Editor Highlights Role of Women in Mexico’s Underworld
articles.description: InSight Crime Managing Editor Deborah Bonello was recently interviewed by the major Mexican newspaper Milenio to discuss her book, “Narcas: The Secret Rise of Women in Latin America’s Cartels,” which was just published in Spanish.

articles.title: Chapitos Plea Deal Raises More Questions on US Role in El Mayo’s Kidnapping in Mexico
articles.description: A top leader of the Chapitos faction of the Sinaloa Cartel has agreed to cooperate with US prosecutors, marking yet another judicial case that will not go to trial or expose the inner workings of organized crime in Mexico.

articles.title: The Drug Case Against the Former Honduras President Trump Pardoned
articles.description: US President Donald Trump’s pardon of former Honduran President Juan Orlando Hernández belies the evidence US prosecutors used to convict the former head of state on cocaine trafficking charges.

articles.title: Juan Orlando Hernández
articles.description: Juan Orlando Hernández, Honduras’ former president, is facing trial on US drug charges after allegations that he received bribes to protect traffickers.

articles.title: Ecuador’s Official Security Claims Belie Reality
articles.description: Ecuador’s government marked two years in office by trumpeting the results of its hardline anti-crime policies. But its claims of progress don’t present a clear picture of the country’s security realities.

articles.title: The Pros and Cons of a US Intervention in Venezuela
articles.description: The world has been watching to see if the United States will escalate its militarized anti-drug mission in the Caribbean into a direct intervention aimed at toppling the regime of  Venezuela’s President Nicolás Maduro, prompting intense debate about the wisdom and potential impacts of such an action

articles.title: How Industrial-Scale Crypto Laundering Sustains Mexico’s Synthetic Drug Trade
articles.description: An exhaustive review of recent US court cases and blockchain data reveals an industrial-scale crypto laundering pipeline moving dirty cash through digital networks.

articles.title: What’s Behind Costa Rica’s ‘First’ Transnational Criminal Organization?
articles.description: Authorities in Costa Rica are on high alert after uncovering the so-called South Caribbean Cartel, reportedly the country’s first transnational criminal organization, which may have severe implications for security policy in the Central American nation amid escalating violence and corruption s

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Adds Nuance to ‘Cartel of the Suns’ Debate
articles.description: After the US State Department announced it would designate Venezuela’s Cartel of the Suns as a “foreign terrorist organization,” InSight Crime Co-director Jeremy McDermott was interviewed by major US news organizations like the New York Times and CNN to clarify what this network wi

articles.title: On the Radar: Rebel Leader Iván Mordisco Threatens Violence After Colombia Military Strikes
articles.description: This week in On the Radar, we analyze the arrest in Spain of one of Ecuador’s most powerful criminal leaders, a major seizure of illegal gold in Paraguay, and the influence of Iván Mordisco, the leader of the Central General Staff, one of the main FARC dissident factions in Colombia.

articles.title: Lobos
articles.description: In the space of just a few years, the Lobos have evolved from a splinter group of the Choneros to one of Ecuador’s most powerful and innovative criminal organizations.

articles.title: How a New Port Created Ecuador’s Latest Criminal Epicenter
articles.description: In 2019, Playas and Posorja recorded just one and three homicides, respectively. But by 2023, homicides had jumped to record highs — 80 in Playas and 75 in Posorja. The surge in criminal activity is related to the opening of a new, deep-water port in Posorja. The port has attracted business from som

articles.title: Top Leader of Ecuador’s Lobos Gang Arrested in Spain
articles.description: In a joint operation, authorities arrested the top leader of the Lobos, Wilmer Chavarría, alias “Pipo,” in the Spanish city of Málaga.

articles.title: Panama Profile
articles.description: Panama’s location connecting Central and South America has made it a key transit point for illicit trade, as well as a refuge and negotiating area for criminal organizations. Its favorable economy and the booming contraband market of the Colón Free Zone (CFZ) have long attracted international

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Analyzes ELN Role in Colombia’s Cocaine Trade
articles.description: InSight Crime Co-director Jeremy McDermott was interviewed by Germany’s DW News to expand on the role of the ELN guerrillas in Colombia’s drug trade.

articles.title: Chile Election Highlights Voters’ Split on Anti-Crime Policies
articles.description: The first round of Chile’s presidential election was marked by divided opinions on how to address insecurity and organized crime.

articles.title: US Foreign Terrorist Designations in Latin America: An Interactive Map
articles.description:
The United States has intensified its tactics for fighting organized crime in Latin America, including designating various Latin American criminal groups as “foreign terrorist organizations” in 2025.  
 This designation allows the United States to take more aggressive actions against the groups it d

articles.title: On the Radar: US Strikes Trigger International Rebuke
articles.description: This week in On the Radar, Ecuador inaugurates a new maximum-security mega-prison, while Colombia and the United Kingdom push back against deadly US anti-drug operations.

articles.title: The Shifting Tides of Drug Trafficking on Venezuela’s Margarita Island
articles.description: The tourist hotspot of Margarita Island, northeast of mainland Venezuela, is not just the country’s “Pearl of the Caribbean” for its idyllic beaches. It is also a strategic hub in drug trafficking routes to the Caribbean and Europe. Local fishers, criminal networks, and even security officials are i

articles.title: How Argentina’s ‘Bribe Books’ Case Could Take Down an Ex-President
articles.description: InSight Crime summarizes the key details of the so-called “Bribe Books” trial, the biggest corruption case in Argentina’s history.

articles.title: On the Radar: Latin America Blurs the Line Between Crime and Terrorism
articles.description: This week, we look at the recent designation of two Brazilian powerhouse gangs as terrorist organizations in Paraguay, the passing of a tough anti-extortion law amid increased violence in Peru, and the further militarization of the fight against illegal mining in Ecuador.

articles.title: InSight Crime Talks Global Supply Chain of Fentanyl
articles.description: InSight Crime investigator Victoria Dittmar spoke about the global supply chain of fentanyl and the growing production, sale, and use of other synthetic opioids around the world.

articles.title: The Pulpos
articles.description: The Pulpos, also known as the Pulpos of Trujillo, are a transnational Peruvian gang dedicated to extortion, kidnapping, and murder-for-hire.

articles.title: Why Mexican Traffickers Are Leaving Zulia, Venezuela
articles.description: Major Mexican drug trafficking networks that used to have a strong presence in the state of Zulia in northwest Venezuela have left as the regime of President Nicolás Maduro has consolidated control over the country’s organized crime landscape. In an increasingly competitive criminal playing field, t

articles.title: Timeline of US Strikes Against Alleged Drug Boats
articles.description: The deadly US military strikes against alleged drug-carrying vessels in international waters mark a quantum leap and an aggressive escalation in US counternarcotics policy, in which drug trafficking is treated as a national security threat equivalent to terrorism. In order to better understand this

articles.title: What’s Missing From the US-China Fentanyl Agreement
articles.description: The United States and China announced they would step up the fight against fentanyl as part of a new trade deal. But it is not clear if the arrangement addresses the role designer precursor chemicals play in fueling the illicit trade.

articles.title: Why Is the US Attacking ‘Cartels’ That Don’t Exist?
articles.description: US authorities have said recent missile strikes against alleged drug vessels in the Caribbean — and now in the Pacific — are targeting “cartels” and “narco-terrorists.” But the reality of these organizations is that they are dispersed networks that resemble most legal global supply chains, making th

articles.title: Video: How Juan Ramón Matta Ballesteros Shaped Honduras’ Drug Trade
articles.description: Honduran drug trafficker Juan Ramón Matta Ballesteros has died in a US prison at age 80, though his drug trafficking legacy lives on. Deputy director Mike LaSusa and investigator Sam Woolston discuss his career and lasting influence in the underworld.

articles.title: On the Radar: Was Brazil’s Favela Police Op a Raid or a Massacre?
articles.description: This week, we hold the line on more military strikes in the Caribbean and the Pacific, mull the conviction of a Bolivian drug czar in the US, and look at the deadliest police raid yet in a Rio favela in Brazil.

articles.title: Ghost Guns Are Increasingly Empowering Brazil’s Powerful Gangs
articles.description: A recent and extremely deadly anti-gang raid in Rio de Janeiro underscores both the escalating firepower of Brazil’s gangs and the growing challenge authorities face as criminal groups diversify their arms supplies. Trends in the use of military firearms by Brazil’s criminal organizations are the fo

articles.title: Chinese Broker’s Arrest Hits Mexico’s Fentanyl Trade Harder Than ‘Kingpin’ Captures
articles.description: A Chinese citizen accused of leading a transnational drug trafficking network with ties to Mexican criminal groups was recaptured and extradited to the United States, highlighting the key role that intermediaries play in global drug supply chains.

articles.title: Can Peru’s New President José Jerí Win His ‘War on Crime’?
articles.description: The “war on crime” proposed by Peru’s new president faces major challenges as he seeks to pull the country out of a spiraling security crisis.

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Weighs In on Trump’s ‘War on Drugs’
articles.description: InSight Crime Co-director Jeremy McDermott provided expert analysis to Deutsche Welle News after President Donald Trump authorized a CIA covert operation against Venezuela

articles.title: Will New Reform Help Mexico Address Money Laundering Challenges?
articles.description: Reforms to Mexico’s anti-money laundering law earlier this year injected fresh hope and a key step forward in the fight against organized crime, but ongoing challenges may hinder progress.

articles.title: Should Economic Thinking Influence LatAm Anti-Crime Policy?
articles.description: Policies to address organized crime should pay more attention to the economic effects of illicit activities and seek to attack crime from a financial front, experts argued in a recent conference.

articles.title: Behind the Curtain: How Trump’s Spat With Colombia Could Impact Anti-Drug Ops
articles.description: InSight Crime’s Managing Editor Deborah Bonello and Co-Director Jeremy McDermott dig into the latest war of words between President Donald Trump and his Colombian counterpart Gustavo Petro.

articles.title: Three Crime Challenges Facing Bolivia’s New President
articles.description: Bolivia elected a new president who will immediately face an acute economic crisis that could limit the government’s ability to tackle organized crime and push more people into criminal activity.

articles.title: On the Radar: Colombia Rejects Tren de Aragua’s Peace Proposal
articles.description: This week, we cover a mass jailbreak in Guatemala that took the authorities months to discover, and Tren de Aragua leader Larry Changa’s rejection from the government of President Gustavo Petro to talk peace.

articles.title: InSight Crime Offers Expert Analysis on Haiti’s Gangs
articles.description: InSight Crime investigator and project manager Alex Papadovassilakis provided expert analysis to CNN on Haitian gangs.

articles.title: Escritório do Crime
articles.description: Escritório do Crime is one of the largest and most powerful militia groups in Brazil’s second-largest city, Rio de Janeiro.

articles.title: Marcos Willians Herbas Camacho, alias ‘Marcola’
articles.description: Marcos Willians Herbas Camacho, alias “Marcola,” has been the top leader of the First Capital Command (Primeiro Comando da Capital – PCC) since 2002.

articles.title: Feeding the Beast: MS13 in Nashville
articles.description: In Nashville, the MS13 took root through the Thompson Place Locos Salvatrucha clique. Fueled by poverty and social exclusion, the group was disorganized, unsophisticated – but extremely violent.

articles.title: Tren de Aragua in Tennessee: A Ghost Train?
articles.description: In Tennessee, warnings about the advance of Tren de Aragua have been driven more by alarmism than by evidence. While the state has only seen a handful of cases, political discourse is amplifying fear and the stigmatizing of the migrant community.

articles.title: The Nashville ICE Raids: Anti-Gang Offensive or Immigration Crackdown?
articles.description: In Nashville, Tennessee, an immigration enforcement operation supposedly targeting areas with gang activity turned into a dragnet for migrants. The sweep left the migrant community gripped by fear and distrust toward authorities.

articles.title: Even as US-Mexico Border Encounters Plummet, Migrants Are Still Vulnerable to Organized Crime
articles.description: The business of smuggling migrants over the US-Mexico border is in flux, but large numbers of migrants stuck in Mexico remain at the mercy of criminal groups.

articles.title: Can Trinidad and Tobago’s New Government Tackle Gang Corruption?
articles.description: Recent measures to root out corruption in public contracts linked to gangs in Trinidad and Tobago could trigger a backlash from criminal organizations that have long profited from community development programs.

articles.title: On the Radar: Elites in the Spotlight for Criminal Ties
articles.description: Two Guyanese gold magnates indicted by a US court for fraud and money laundering, a member of Argentine President Javier Milei’s party who quit the midterm election race after admitting that he received $200,000 from an aviation tycoon wanted for drug trafficking, and the extradition from Costa Rica

articles.title: Is the Jalisco Cartel Helping the Chapitos in Mexico?
articles.description:
Reports that the Jalisco Cartel New Generation is aiding the 
Chapitos faction of Sinaloa Cartel in parts of Mexico have been growing. 
InSight Crime’s Managing Editor Deborah Bonello and Senior Investigator Parker Asmann discuss the criminal alliance, its structure, and how it contradicts some of t

articles.title: Mexico Doubles Down on Militarization With National Guard Reform
articles.description: As part of a series of security reforms pushed by Mexico’s government, the country’s main civilian public security force has been placed under military command, extending the long-standing trend of militarization in the fight against crime. InSight Crime spoke with Daira Arana, executive director of

articles.title: From the Caribbean to the Pacific: Piracy on the Rise in Latin America
articles.description: In the late 17th century, Caribbean pirates hunted galleons loaded with gold and silver bound for Europe. Today, things have changed — but not much. Fast boats slip out at night, men climb aboard with machetes in hand, and terrified crews stay silent. The treasure chests are gone, replaced by fuel,

articles.title: On the Radar: Are the Chapitos and CJNG Working Together in Mexico?
articles.description: This week, we look at a massive DEA sweep against alleged Jalisco Cartel operatives in the US, growing signs of an alliance between Mexico’s two major criminal powers, and an assassination attempt in Uruguay connected to international trafficker Sebastián Marset.

articles.title: Triple Murder Streamed Online Underscores Fears of Escalating Violence in Argentina
articles.description: The livestreamed torture and killing of three women at the hands of a drug gang has rattled Argentina and underscored the country’s vulnerability to extreme drug-fueled violence. While broadcast killings are rare in Argentina, drug trafficking groups in other Latin American countries have used

articles.title: InSight Crime Is Finalist in International Investigative Journalism Festival
articles.description: From September 24–28, InSight Crime participated in the 11th edition of the DIG Awards, an international festival of investigative journalism in Modena, Italy, where our Project Manager James Bargent was […]

articles.title: Behind the Curtain: If Venezuela’s Regime Changed, What Could It Mean for Organized Crime?
articles.description: Why you should care about tensions between the Trump and Maduro administrations, what a power vacuum could mean for Venezuela’s armed groups, and the impact of more US military action on the international cocaine business: InSight Crime unpacks current tensions in the Caribbean.

articles.title: US Drone Strike Highlights Sucre’s Role in Venezuela’s Cocaine Corridor
articles.description: On September 2, a small boat left the coast of Sucre state, Venezuela, and headed into the Caribbean Sea. articles.title: Uruguay’s Acting AG Attacked at Home After Marset-Linked Bust
articles.description: Uruguay has rarely suffered attacks against officials by organized crime, but as the country plays an increasingly important role in the international cocaine trade, global criminal networks have a greater incentive to use violence to protect their illicit activities.

articles.title: 5 New Creative Ways Traffickers Tried to Smuggle Cocaine
articles.description: The endless growth of the global cocaine trade and the game of whack-a-mole to stop it has propelled innovation and experimentation from drug trafficking organizations to get their product out into the world. Hiding cocaine in banana shipments, hollowed-out lumber, and soy flour have become popular

articles.title: Local Media References InSight Crime’s Knowledge of Tren de Aragua
articles.description: On the two-year anniversary of the Venezuelan government’s announcement that Tren de Aragua had been “totally dismantled” following its takeover of the group’s headquarters in the Tocorón prison, regional outlets […]

articles.title: How Fuel Theft Drives Mexico’s Violence Epidemic
articles.description: Fuel theft has become a catalyst for some of Mexico’s worst violence. Fuel theft gangs must use force to control territory close to pipelines and other oil infrastructure in order to access fuel supplies. Turf disputes commonly spark conflicts between rival groups. As huachicol has expanded into one

articles.title: On the Radar: US Designates Barrio 18 a Terrorist Group
articles.description: This week, we analyze whether the new US terrorist designation for the Barrio 18 gang is political or practical, another prison riot in Ecuador, and how AI is helping organized crime in Mexico.

articles.title: Ghost Riders and Deepfake Doctors: Inside Brazil’s AI-Driven Crime Surge
articles.description: Brazil’s criminal groups are weaponizing artificial intelligence (AI) to drive high-tech financial fraud, exploiting gaps in a fast-growing digital ecosystem as banks, legislators, and police scramble to keep up. InSight Crime highlights three recent cases that show how Brazil’s widespread connectiv

articles.title: Diesel Subsidy Cut Could Make Fuel Theft Worse in Ecuador
articles.description: Eliminating the diesel subsidy cuts opportunities for smugglers who buy cheap fuel in Ecuador to sell in neighboring countries, but it increases incentives for criminal groups to steal fuel that they use in various illegal activities. Fuel theft is already rising. State-owned oil giant Petroecuador

articles.title: Santa Rosa de Lima Cartel (CSRL)
articles.description:
The Santa Rosa de Lima Cartel (CSRL) emerged in Guanajuato as a network of gangs dedicated to fuel theft and became one of the state’s deadliest drivers of violence. 
Despite government crackdowns and the advance of the CJNG, the CSRL continues to control key criminal economies such as fuel theft, e

articles.title: InSight Crime Provides Expert Analysis on US Airstrike
articles.description: As international attention turns to the US government’s escalating War on Drugs policies, InSight Crime’s Deputy Director of Content Mike LaSusa joined the “Angry Planet” podcast to provide expert analysis […]

articles.title: Venezuela Is So Corrupt That Criminals Are Impersonating Government Officials
articles.description: At least seven people have been arrested in Venezuela this year for posing as corrupt officials from the justice system, police and administrative bodies, showing how entrenched corruption has turned public administration into an opportunity for organized crime.

articles.title: On the Radar: Seismic US Drug War Shifts
articles.description: This week, we analyze another US missile strike in the region, what the decertification of Colombia in the US war on drugs means, and the sentencing of seven former FARC leaders for kidnapping and war crimes during decades of armed conflict.

articles.title: US Pressures Colombia With Threat of Anti-Drug Aid Cuts
articles.description: The US government has added Colombia to a list of nations it says are failing to cooperate in the fight against drug trafficking, but for now US anti-narcotics assistance will continue to flow to the Andean nation.

articles.title: Is Rosario, Argentina Getting Safer? Depends Who We Asked.
articles.description: Last year was the least deadly on record for Argentina’s city of Rosario in a decade. But if all is calm in the wealthy center, the gang wars on its outskirts remain.

articles.title: Behind Bars in Argentina’s Organized Crime Hotspot
articles.description: In a wing reserved for members of Rosario’s most violent gang, the Monos, InSight Crime watches a “pigeon” moving back and forth between cells. Made up of torn sheets, plastic […]

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-Director Participates in Security Policy Roundtable
articles.description: InSight Crime’s expertise on organized crime and public security was featured this week when co-director Steven Dudley participated in the Jornadas Internacionales de Seguridad en Ecuador: Retos y Soluciones in […]

articles.title: Historic PCC Bust Spurs Brazil Crackdown on Digital Money Laundering
articles.description: An investigation into a multibillion-dollar First Capital Command money laundering scheme involving chemical imports, fuel distribution, and investment funds has triggered reforms in Brazil’s digital banking sector. articles.title: On the Radar: Crime Crackdowns Sweep LatAm
articles.description: This week we question the nature of Venezuela’s Cartel of the Suns, Tren de Aragua, and US military ops in the Caribbean; ask whether a crackdown on the Border Command on Ecuador’s border will slow money laundering; and ponder the future of Haiti’s anti-gang mission.

articles.title: Behind the Curtain: Venezuela’s ‘Cartels’ and the US Missile Strike Explained
articles.description: The targeting of a go-fast boat off the coast of Venezuela on September 2 heralded a quantum shift in the US war on drugs from South America. But who are the players and what will the impact be of the killing of 11 alleged drug traffickers?

articles.description: Powerful criminal groups in Mexico are expanding their involvement in fuel theft, one of the country’s fastest-growing and most profitable criminal economies

articles.title: 5 Things to Know About Trump’s Missile Strike in the Caribbean
articles.description: The United States has significantly escalated its policy of treating organized crime groups as terrorists with a lethal missile strike on an alleged drug trafficking boat in the Caribbean. The White House has provided few details about the strike, which raises several strategic and operational quest

articles.title: New Charges in Emblematic Murder Case Shake Ecuador’s Political Landscape
articles.description: Ecuadorian prosecutors have charged former Interior Minister José Serrano with helping plan the high-profile assassination of a presidential candidate in 2023, marking a crucial moment in the country’s fight against corruption. But it could also mark what critics have called a dangerous politicizati

articles.title: Video: Are President Bukele’s Celebrations Around Security Gains Justified?
articles.description: President Nayib Bukele in El Salvador is celebrating 1,000 days without homicides since he took power in 2019. articles.title: Video: The US Just Bombed a ‘Narco Terrorist’ Boat. Or Did It?
articles.description: A US military airstrike in the southern Caribbean’s international waters, which the Trump administration claims killed 11 members of the Tren de Aragua “narco-terrorist organization,” is a concerning, deadly escalation in the current “war on drugs.” We debunk the myths that the Tren de Aragua is eit

articles.title: How Mexico’s New Intelligence Law Will Impact Public Security
articles.description: On July 1, 2025, Mexico approved the Law of the National System of Investigation and Intelligence in Public Security. The new legislation, driven by President Claudia Sheinbaum’s government, creates a legal framework for coordination among security agencies tackling high-impact crimes.

articles.title: Colombia Blames Guerrillas For Senator’s Killing, But With Little Evidence
articles.description: The sentencing of a minor for the fatal shooting of Colombian senator and presidential hopeful Miguel Uribe Turbay has underscored the government’s focus on a group of guerrillas as the alleged masterminds, though evidence remains thin.

articles.title: InSight Crime Explains the Sinaloa Cartel’s Internal War on Spanish TV
articles.description: InSight Crime investigator and project manager Victoria Dittmar provided expert analysis on the Spanish TV show laSexta about the ongoing factional war in Sinaloa, highlighting how the conflict between the […]

articles.title: The Chapitos: Bloodlines and Battlefields in Mexico’s Sinaloa Cartel
articles.description:
The state of Sinaloa, Mexico is paralyzed by a blood feud between two crime families. 
 The two sides? The Chapitos and the Mayiza, two factions of the Sinaloa Cartel, each led by the sons of a different founder. 
 These “narco juniors” grew up side by side, learning the drug business from a young a

articles.title: On the Radar: El Mayo’s Plea Will Change Nothing
articles.description: This week’s On the Radar handles El Mayo’s guilty plea, tensions in Colombia’s peace process, and the US’s latest military move.

articles.title: The New Face of Venezuela’s ‘Pranato’ System
articles.description: The system of criminal leadership in Venezuelan prisons, better known as “pranato,” refuses to die despite the government operations that have hit it hard. According to the latest report from the Prison Observatory (Observatorio de Prisiones – OVP), criminals who began dominating p

articles.title: Brazil’s Red Command Turned Turf Control Into Ride-Hailing Monopoly
articles.description: Brazil’s Red Command gang created its own Uber-style ride-hailing app, showing how Brazilian gangs are combining real-world territorial control and increasing technological sophistication to generate new revenue streams. The Red Command managed its app, called Rotax Mobili, together with an allied c

articles.title: Beyond Tren de Aragua: Venezuelan Gangs Spread Across Latin America
articles.description: While headlines and political speeches focus predominately on Tren de Aragua, another criminal group from Venezuela, the Zulia gangs, has expanded its operations in Latin America, leaving a quieter but no less violent mark than the more famous Venezuelan crime group.

articles.title: La Línea
articles.description: La Línea is one of the top criminal forces in the Mexican state of Chihuahua, engaging in everything from migrant smuggling to drug trafficking. Its home base is Ciudad Juárez, from which it projects its power along an important stretch of the US-Mexico border.

articles.title: On the Radar: ‘El Mayo’ To Plead Guilty
articles.description: This week, On The Radar focuses on Sinaloa Cartel co-founder Ismael Zambada Garcia, alias “El Mayo,” who is set to plead guilty to multiple drug trafficking charges in US federal court, the presidential race in Bolivia, which could reshape the country’s approach to cocaine production, and how in Guy

articles.title: Tren de Aragua Investigation Findings Make Headlines Across the Region
articles.description: InSight Crime published its latest investigation on Tren de Aragua this week: Tren de Aragua: Fact vs. Fiction. The findings, from three years of on-the-ground reporting across various countries, garnered […]

articles.title: Chiquito Malo: Warlord of Colombia’s AGC Crime Conglomerate
articles.description:
Who is Chiquito Malo?  
 The top leader of the Gaitanistas, one of Colombia’s largest criminal structures, with a presence in more than half of Colombia’s departments.  
 A criminal mastermind, who has directed the Gaitanistas’ war with Colombian armed groups and managed its international alliances

articles.title: Second Marquetalia
articles.description: The Second Marquetalia is one of the largest group of dissident FARC, who rejected the 2016 peace process with the government.

articles.title: Video: The Military is Going to Leave Ecuador’s Prisons – What Comes Next?
articles.description: Last week, Ecuador announced it would begin to withdraw military forces from the prisons, over a year and half after deploying them to break criminal control. The news marks the beginning of the end of a policy that has been an inflection point in the country’s criminal history.

articles.title: What Is Tren de Aragua?
articles.description: Is Tren de Aragua a terrorist organization bent on invading the United States or a spent criminal force being used as an excuse to target the Venezuelan regime?

articles.title: The Tocorón Takeover
articles.description: Tocorón Prison was the most notorious in the country and the main sanctuary and center of operations of Tren de Aragua.

articles.title: Bolívar: A New Sanctuary for Tren de Aragua?
articles.description: In Bolívar, Tren de Aragua does not operate like the prison-based megabanda that expanded across the region. Instead, it functions as a sindicato – the term used for criminal groups involved in illegal mining in Venezuela.

articles.title: Tren de Aragua Clashes with Colombia’s Underworld
articles.description: Colombia has proven to be fertile territory for Tren de Aragua’s operations, expanding its criminal rents and offering refuge to some of its most notorious leaders.

articles.title: How Tren de Aragua Became Peru’s ‘Public Enemy Number One’
articles.description: Remaining low profile limited Tren de Aragua’s ability to establish itself in Peru. So the group began to expand into zones claimed by other criminal groups.

articles.title: Safe Chile Meets Extreme Gang Violence
articles.description: In Chile, elements linked to Tren de Aragua found a country that had not experienced anything like the transnational crime group, and the Venezuelan gang used that lack of experience to its advantage.

articles.title: Tren de Aragua and the American Dream
articles.description: Tren de Aragua has little chance of building criminal power in the United States with so much law enforcement and public attention focused on it.

articles.title: The Future of Tren de Aragua
articles.description: The Tren de Aragua brand is far stronger than the organization behind it, fed in no small part by discourse coming out of the United States. But what is the future of the gang and Venezuelan transnational organized crime?

articles.title: Separating Fact From Fiction With Tren de Aragua
articles.description: Even as the infamy of the Tren de Aragua grows in the Americas, its cohesion and organization are weakening.

articles.title: Las Claritas Sindicato
articles.description: Las Claritas Sindicato is one of the strongest and longest-lasting criminal groups dedicated to illegal mining in Venezuela’s southern state of Bolívar.

articles.title: Bolivia’s Election Could Reshape Coca Policy
articles.description: Bolivians will vote Sunday in a presidential election pitting candidates promising tougher anti-drug measures against defenders of the country’s nationalist coca policies. The result could reshape how the Andean nation addresses cocaine production, organized crime, and protection of the Amazon.

articles.title: InSight Crime Analyzes Increasingly Militarized US Anti-Drug Approach
articles.description: InSight Crime investigator and project manager Victoria Dittmar provided expert analysis to Bloomberg on how the US, under President Donald Trump, is adopting a confrontational stance toward Latin America that […]

articles.title: Niño Guerrero: The Prison Kingpin Who Took Tren de Aragua Transnational
articles.description:
On September 20, 2023, 11,000 Venezuelan police officers and soldiers were deployed to Tocorón prison in the state of Aragua. 
 The goal? To take back control of the prison from the criminal group, Tren de Aragua, which had its headquarters there. 
 What they found inside was far from a normal priso

articles.title: As Migrant Flows Drop, Crime Groups Adapt in Ciudad Juárez
articles.description: With the tightening of US migration policy after Donald Trump, the number of migrants seeking to cross the US/Mexico border has dropped sharply. With shelters nearly empty, criminal groups are losing prey and a major source of criminal income, and being forced to adapt.

articles.title: The Crime Wars Driving Rising Homicides in Ceará, Brazil
articles.description: Local and national criminal networks battling over drug routes and markets have turned Brazil’s northeastern state of Ceará into one of the country’s main epicenters of violence.

articles.title: Trump Official Headed Organization That Spread Fake News About Tren de Aragua
articles.description: An official recently appointed to a leadership post in the US Department of Defense previously led a think tank that published false reports of Tren de Aragua activities in the United States, contributing to misinformation about the gang.

articles.title: Video: Will El Salvador Reforms Help or Hinder Bukele’s Anti-Crime Fight?
articles.description: What does the end of presidential term limits in El Salvador mean for the country’s fight against organized crime and the rule of law?

articles.title: Video: What Trump’s Directive to Target Foreign Drug Cartels With the US Military Could Mean
articles.description: InSight Crime’s co-director Steven Dudley and managing editor Deborah Bonello discuss President Trump’s latest directive, and why it fundamentally misunderstands the region’s criminal landscape.

articles.title: Colombia-Venezuela Border Killing Hints at Growing Guerrilla Conflict
articles.description: The alleged killing of a guerrilla commander near the Colombia-Venezuela border suggests that a fragile criminal alliance that up until now had regulated several illicit economies like illegal mining in this region has fractured.

articles.title: InSight Crime Adds Nuance to US-Mexico Border Conversation
articles.description: InSight Crime investigator and project manager Victoria Dittmar provided expert analysis to the Texas Observer for an article that examined how US-Mexico border strategies often fall short in tackling the […]

articles.title: In Ciudad Juárez, Mexico, Old Criminal Groups Form New Criminal Guard
articles.description: Recent fighting in Ciudad Juárez appears to be a reflection of a criminal terrain in flux, one in which an old criminal guard is being replaced by a new one amid a changing criminal economy.

articles.title: Fito: The Rise and Fall of Ecuador’s Most Wanted Crime Boss
articles.description:
The Ecuadorian crime boss has been all over news headlines this year. 
 On March 28, US federal prosecutors filed drug trafficking and gun charges against Fito. At the time, he was on the run after escaping prison in January 2024.  
 Then, on June 25, he was captured in Montecristi, a town just outs

articles.title: Gangs, Tattoos, and the Roots of the US Due Process Crisis
articles.description: Since the start of the year, cases of Latin American immigrants judged to be gang members for little more than tattoos have made news thanks to the mass deportation campaigns of US President Donald Trump.

articles.title: Is Latin America Prepared to Mitigate Digital Recruitment by Crime Groups?

articles.description: In June 2024, Guatemala’s Interior Ministry filed a legal complaint with the Attorney General’s Office against 102 prison guards it accused of corruption. But prosecutors are yet to mount a robust investigation into the case, and they may have little incentive to do so.

Extortion appears to be increasing in Honduras as smaller gangs fill the gap left by larger groups who are retreating from the criminal economy.  
 At the same time, arrests for extortion are falling, indicating growing impunity for this predatory criminal activity.
