articles.title: The PCC Plays Its Dirty Game in Portuguese Football
articles.description: Evidence linking Brazil’s biggest organized crime network and Portuguese football highlights how sports can serve as a vehicle for money laundering.

articles.title: The Rise and Fall of El Negro Willy, Ecuador’s Prison-Guard Mafia Boss
articles.description: Willy was born in 1989. He grew up in Esmeraldas, a Pacific coast city near Ecuador’s northwestern border with Colombia that is rich in Afro-Ecuadorian culture but plagued by underdevelopment and high unemployment. His criminal career, however, took off in Guayaquil, Ecuador’s largest city.

articles.title: Fito’s Fortress: How an Ecuador Prison Became a Criminal Command Center
articles.description: Fito had escaped from a maximum-security facility for the second time in ten years. But unlike 2013, when he and 17 more of Ecuador’s most dangerous criminals staged a cinematic jailbreak, this time he left no trace: no handcuffed guards or cut fences; no footprints or boats lying in wait. He had si

articles.title: Rasquiña’s Revolution: How the Choneros Took Ecuador’s Prisons
articles.description: Rasquiña was born in Manta, a port city in the central province of Manabí. There, as a young man, he met two people who would change the trajectory of his life: Jorge Bismarck Véliz España, a.k.a. “Teniente España,” an up-and-coming criminal from the nearby town of Chone, and José Adolfo Macías Vill

articles.title: Jailhouse Massacre in Ecuador Illustrates Rapid Criminal Evolution
articles.description: A recent massacre inside Ecuador’s largest and most violent prison is an ominous sign that the country’s multiple criminal organizations are atomizing at a rapid pace as the government’s hardline security crackdown stalls.

articles.title: What an Internal Split Means for Colombia’s Second Marquetalia
articles.description: A recent rift within the Second Marquetalia underscores the inherent fragility of alliances among illegal groups and highlights the significant challenges the Colombian government faces when negotiating with organizations of diverse and fragmented origins.

articles.title: Dominican Republic Breaks Seizure Record Amid Renewed Caribbean Trafficking
articles.description: Record-breaking cocaine seizures in the Dominican Republic highlight the resurgence of the Caribbean as a major trafficking route to Europe amid unprecedented cocaine production in Colombia.

articles.title: Haiti Gangs Push For Power Amid Political Chaos
articles.description: Gangs in Haiti are exploiting political turmoil to tighten their grip on the country, paralyzing domestic and international efforts to restore law and order.

articles.title: Colombia Nabs Another Top Tren de Aragua Leader With Venezuela’s Help
articles.description: The arrest of a high-ranking Tren de Aragua member marks another significant blow to the gang’s operations in Colombia and offers further proof that the protection it once received from Venezuelan authorities is long gone.

articles.title: Murder at Brazil’s Busiest Airport Exposes Failures in Witness Protection
articles.description: The assassination of a police informant in São Paulo’s main international airport has exposed serious flaws in Brazil’s witness protection system, complicating efforts to dismantle powerful organized crime networks.

articles.title: InSight Crime Wins Colombia’s Simon Bolívar Award
articles.description: InSight Crime has won the Simón Bolívar National Journalism Prize in the category of investigative journalism for the text publication “The Informants of Tibú: How the Colombian State Unleashed a Wave of Femicides.”

articles.title: Smuggling Adapts to Reopening of Colombia-Venezuela Border
articles.description: As darkness falls over Cúcuta, Colombia’s bustling border city, shadows provide cover for criminal networks. Each night, illegal dirt tracks connecting the city to Venezuela become highways for smuggling, with large quantities of goods slipping past law enforcement.

articles.title: The Rise of Global Crime Networks: European Mafias in the Americas
articles.description: The expansion of foreign criminal groups into Latin America has taken on a new dimension in the last decade, with European networks directly organizing drug shipments from the region.

articles.title: How ‘Canteens’ Feed Crime in Brazil’s Prisons
articles.description: The dismantling of the “canteen mafia,” which controlled food distribution in Rio de Janeiro’s prisons, highlights the growing presence of smaller, niche criminal enterprises within Brazil’s penitentiary system.

articles.title: Despite Interventions, Venezuela’s Prison Gangs Thrive
articles.description: The intervention in yet another Venezuelan prison under criminal leaders’ control highlights the ongoing failure of President Nicolás Maduro’s regime to eliminate the country’s prison gangs despite a year of targeted operations.

articles.title: Chile’s Arica Port Bust Underscores Role in Bolivian Cocaine Supply Chain
articles.description: The massive recent seizure of cocaine precursor at Chile’s Arica port underscores its growing role as a trafficking hotspot for Bolivian drug networks.

articles.title: Turkish Broker for Sanction-Busting Venezuelan Oil Arrested
articles.description: The recent arrest of a prominent Turkish broker of Venezuelan oil in the United States may signal turbulent times ahead for Nicolás Maduro’s administration following Donald Trump’s re-election.

articles.title: Earnings From Illegal Gold Hit Record High in Peru
articles.description: The earnings from Illegally extracted gold in Peru have soared to record levels, fueling criminal organizations while the country debates whether to extend its mining formalization efforts.

articles.title: Head of Argentina’s Monos Gang Racks up 160 Years in Sentences
articles.description: The leader of Argentina’s most notorious criminal group may see another sentence of 25 years added to his tally, although this will do little to stifle the gang’s activities or his leadership.

articles.title: Will Costa Rica Follow the Same Criminal Path as Ecuador?
articles.description: High levels of violence indicate how once-peaceful Costa Rica is feeling the tightening grip of cocaine trafficking. But is the Central American country on a similar path to Ecuador to the south?

articles.title: Popular Election of Judges in Mexico: Risk of Corruption and Inefficiency
articles.description: Mexico’s newly approved judicial reform will see over 1,600 federal judges chosen by popular vote, sparking concerns over the judiciary’s vulnerability to organized crime.

articles.title: United States Targets Mexico’s the Línea Gang Over Fentanyl
articles.description: The United States government has sanctioned members of Mexico’s the Línea criminal group for their involvement in fentanyl trafficking, again revealing the importance of this deadly trade to Washington.

articles.title: Two US-Mexico Border Sheriffs, Two Visions on Immigration and Security
articles.description: As US voters prepare to select their next president, few issues are more important to the electorate than the immigration and crime debate.

articles.title: The Doubts Behind Bolivia’s ‘Biggest Cocaine Seizure Ever’
articles.description:
The announcement of a 21-ton seizure of cocaine in Bolivia, if true, reinforces the country’s role as a drug producer and transit hub. 
 But unless each bag of flour contained pure cocaine, the seizure is unlikely to have amounted to so much and raises questions about the reliability of offici

articles.title: No Limit Soldiers
articles.description: The No Limit Soldiers (NLS) is a crime group that started out as a neighborhood gang in Curaçao. The organization’s leadership set up a cocaine trafficking route to the Netherlands in the early 2000s.

articles.title: Italy’s Camorra Moves Into Colombia’s Cocaine Capital
articles.description:
Two high-ranking members of Italy’s Camorra were arrested in Medellín, Colombia, in a multinational operation against cocaine trafficking. 
 The Camorra’s presence in Medellín is not an isolated trend, and is a response to a shift in the power structures in the Colombian drug trade in re

articles.title: Spain Reclaims Position as Cocaine Gateway to Europe
articles.description:
A record 13-ton seizure of cocaine in Spain is evidence that the country has bounced back as the main entry point into Europe for the drug.    
 The development comes after Belgium and the Netherlands significantly improved security at their ports.

articles.title: Feared Zetas Brothers Reign from Mexico Prisons: US Prosecutors
articles.description:
Authorities in the United States have accused the feared Treviño Morales brothers of continuing to control remnants of the Zetas despite a decade behind bars in Mexico.    
 The new superseding indictment raises questions as to why the infamous criminal leaders have yet to be extradited.

articles.title: People Are Overdosing on Fentanyl Pills Bought From Fake Online Pharmacies
articles.description:
US authorities recently targeted a drug trafficking ring that set up fake online pharmacies selling counterfeit prescription pills laced with fentanyl and methamphetamine.    
 The case underscores the growing sophistication of such distribution networks and the challenges of combating them.

articles.title: Cash and Violence: Organized Crime Fights to Control Brazil’s Municipal Vote
articles.description:
With the second round of Brazil’s municipal elections approaching, criminal groups are increasingly being caught using new financial methods to sway results in addition to violence. 
 Brazilian lawmakers reported an unprecedented volume of corruption, vote buying, and organized crime infiltration du

articles.title: Crime, Drugs, and Violence Top Concerns in Uruguay’s Election
articles.description:
As Uruguayans prepare to vote in the first round of presidential elections this weekend, security has emerged as a pivotal issue.  
 Candidates are shifting toward the center and debating whether prevention or suppression offers the best solution to escalating crime.

articles.title: Tiguerones
articles.description: Within just a few years, the Tiguerones have risen from obscurity to a position as one of Ecuador’s most dangerous and influential criminal organizations and an important link in the international cocaine trafficking supply chain.

articles.title: Arrest of Leaders of Ecuador’s Tiguerones Could Further Fracture Gang
articles.description:
The leader of the Tiguerones, one of Ecuador’s most powerful and violent criminal groups and an important link in the international cocaine supply chain, has been captured in Spain, potentially hastening the group’s fragmentation.  
 William Alcívar Bautista, alias “Willy” or “Negro Willy,” was take

articles.title: Ecuador Is Coca Producer Country, Says President Noboa
articles.description: Cultivators are growing about 2,000 hectares of coca crops in Ecuador, President Daniel Noboa announced in an October 14 address circulated on local TV stations. The figure, which is the first official estimate of coca crop cultivation in Ecuador, highlights the country’s role as a fledgling cocaine

articles.title: Historic Guilty Plea by Bank Highlights US Crackdown on Money Launderers
articles.description:
In a historic first, a US bank has admitted to helping criminal networks in Colombia launder hundreds of millions of dollars in dirty money.    
 The news highlights the fruits of a renewed push from US law enforcement to target those aiding the region’s money launderers.

articles.title: Modern Slavery in Brazil Is Targeting More Women, More Foreigners
articles.description: Brazil has seen a surge in the number of foreign nationals and women rescued from forced labor conditions, a form of human trafficking also known as modern slavery, according to a new report. The annual human trafficking report from the Ministry of Justice and Public Security covers the evolution of

articles.title: Colombia’s Coca Crops Grew, But Cocaine Production Exploded, Latest Figures Show
articles.description: Coca crops and cocaine production in Colombia increased during 2023, as the government has stopped forced eradication efforts and concentrated on seizures to fight drug trafficking.

articles.title: Is Mexico Fueling a Gang War by Sending Migrants to Tabasco?
articles.description: Violence is spiking in the southern Mexican state, and an unofficial policy is funneling more vulnerable migrants there, right into the crosshairs of organized crime.

articles.title: Mexico’s Former Security Chief Just Got Decades in Prison. But Will That Curtail Cartel Corruption?
articles.description: Genaro García Luna is the latest Mexican security official to be prosecuted in the United States, but it is unclear if prosecutions of former US partners have done anything to stymie corruption south of the border.

articles.title: Power Play or Peace? AGC’s Role in Colombia’s Fragile Negotiations
articles.description:
The strength of Colombia’s AGC will pose a serious challenge in nascent peace negotiations, according to a recent report. 
 And although starting peace talks with the group is a major step forward for President Gustavo Petro’s “Total Peace” plan, further progress will prove challenging.

articles.title: Why Do Latin American Governments Keep Betting on States of Emergency?
articles.description:
Several Latin American countries have recently declared or prolonged states of emergency aimed at combating organized crime.  
 InSight Crime asks why, if the results of these efforts have so far been lackluster.

articles.title: From Fame to Fraud: How Argentina’s Influencers Help Illegal Gambling
articles.description: A cyber crime group in Argentina used social media influencers to legitimize its illegal gambling websites seeking to launder money. The government raid on the group highlights the growing variety of crimes committed with the help of common technology.

articles.title: Mexico’s Narco Tanks Are Going From Impractical to Tactical
articles.description: The use of Mexico’s now-legendary narco tanks in face-offs between criminal groups is growing. Technological innovations have helped them evolve from being a symbolic emblem to an important tactical weapon on the battlefield.

articles.title: Conquering Self Defense Forces of the Sierra Nevada (ACSN)
articles.description: The Conquering Self Defense Forces of the Sierra Nevada (Autodefensas Conquistadoras de la Sierra Nevada – ACSN), also known as Los Pachenca, is an armed group that emerged following the demobilization in 2006 of the paramilitary army led by Hernán Giraldo, alias “El Patrón,” on Colombia’s Car

articles.title: DEA Under Fire for Delaying Reports on Foreign Partners’ Security Abuses
articles.description:
The DEA repeatedly delayed reporting human rights violations by the Latin American security forces it was partnering with, adding to a series of scandals at the world’s most influential anti-drug agency. 
 The newest revelations about the beleaguered agency could mean that the United States is activ

articles.title: Larry Amaury Álvarez Núñez, alias ‘Larry Changa’
articles.description: Larry Amaury Álvarez Núñez, alias “Larry Changa,” is one of the co-founders and original leaders of Tren de Aragua, Venezuela’s most prominent criminal organization, which has expanded its operations across Latin America.

articles.title: Violence Against Women Is Being Used as a Weapon of War in Colombia. Again.
articles.description:
In Colombia, local criminal gangs have put women in their crosshairs because of their links to rival groups, resorting to gender-based violence as a strategy for territorial control. 
 The AGC’s “Plan Feminicidio” shows, once again, how criminal groups use violence against women as a weapon of war i

articles.title: InSight Crime Receives Special Citation by the Maria Moors Cabot Prize Jury
articles.description: InSight Crime traveled to New York to receive the Maria Moors Cabot Prize, which recognizes 15 years of work in deepening the understanding of organized crime and its impact on the Americas.

articles.title: Mayor’s Beheading Is Ominous Sign of Security Crisis Facing Mexico’s New President
articles.description:
The beheading of the mayor of a major Mexican city just days after taking office has put a spotlight on the country’s dire security crisis. 
 Mexico’s newly-installed President Claudia Sheinbaum faces a formidable task in reversing the disintegration in public security that the country has wit

articles.title: Borders Are No Barrier to Booming Cyber Crime, and Authorities Must Adapt
articles.description: Cyber crime in Latin America is growing, and its use of technology knows no borders. Law enforcement is having to adapt to its transnational nature, and InSight Crime spoke with the head of Ameripol’s Cyber Crime Specialized Center about the organizations’ achievements and the challenges it fa

articles.title: What We Know About Tren de Aragua’s US Presence
articles.description:
Tren de Aragua’s supposed expansion into the United States has received national media coverage and attention from the country’s most prominent politicians, fueling questions about the extent of the group’s presence and activities. 
 But the gang’s reputation appears to have grown more quickly than

articles.title: Migrant Smugglers Threaten Panama’s Darién Conservation Plans
articles.description: Panama has announced an initiative to combat environmental degradation in the Darién Gap, but entrenched criminal groups in the region will make these measures difficult to implement.

articles.title: Uruguay Faces Rising Threat From Organized Crime
articles.description: A series of recent criminal activities suggests that organized crime has set its sights on Uruguay and reveals the vulnerabilities in what has historically been one of the most prosperous and well-governed countries in the region.

articles.title: El Salvador Tops Security Perceptions Survey as Chile, Ecuador Decline
articles.description: The Gallup Global Safety Report 2024 administers and analyzes a poll evaluating citizens’ experiences of violent crime (robberies, assaults, muggings), their perception of safety in general, and their trust in law enforcement over the past year. According to the report, people in Latin America and t

articles.title: Tren de Guayana Gang Expands Into Southern Venezuela Mining Towns
articles.description: Tren de Guayana, one of Venezuela’s oldest illegal mining groups, allegedly has used ties to President Nicolás Maduro’s administration to push out other criminal groups and solidify its power in the state of Bolívar.

articles.title: Criminals Set Their Sights on Peru’s Cultural Legacy
articles.description: The looting of archaeological heritage and land trafficking in Peru is reaching alarming levels, and authorities lack the resources and will to combat the criminal networks behind this trade. Growing international demand, mainly from collectors in Europe and North America is driving the looting of a

articles.title: InSight Crime is a leading expert source on Tren de Aragua’s presence in the US
articles.description: InSight Crime continues to be a leading source of information and analysis on the presence of the Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua in the United States. On September 20, deputy […]

articles.title: The Rise and Fall of Washo, Durán’s Criminal Puppetmaster
articles.description: In the early hours of August 14, Ecuador’s police and prosecutors deployed one of the most significant security operations of recent years in and around Durán, a coastal municipality that has become an epicenter of violence in the country.

articles.title: The Anatomy of Violence in Durán
articles.description: Homicides are a manifestation of increasing conflict between criminal groups in Durán. In this chapter, we will use data from Ecuador’s Ministry of the Interior to first explore the main features of homicides in Ecuador, conduct an in-depth analysis of homicides in Durán and how they compare to the

articles.title: From Gangs to Gatekeepers: Criminal Capital in Durán
articles.description: Durán’s street gangs are vilified for their current role in violence and criminality in the municipality, but their relationship with the communities in which they operate is complicated. Some gang leaders invest in social capital by filling in for absent, corrupt, and inept state institutions to pr

articles.title: The Web of Illicit Economies Ensnaring Durán, Ecuador
articles.description: At the heart of Durán’s transformation into Ecuador’s epicenter of organized crime lies a complex web of interconnected criminal economies. These illicit activities have not only reshaped the municipality’s landscape but have also become deeply intertwined with its social, economic

articles.title: Kings, Killers, and Cabras: Profiling Durán’s Criminal Players
articles.description: Durán’s criminal underworld is dominated by the fierce rivalry between the Latin Kings and the Chone Killers. Their differences — in organizational structure, leadership style, and historical roots — reflect the complexity of Durán’s criminal ecosystem. Further complicating this landscape is a third

articles.title: Tapestry of Crime and Complicity: Mapping Durán’s Power Players
articles.description: A multitude of actors play a role in security in Durán, including political, bureaucratic, and economic elites, civil society, and security forces. Corruption in the municipal government, including in the mayor’s office and various other key posts, has allowed organized crime groups to flourish. Thi

articles.title: A Short History of Durán, Ecuador
articles.description: Durán was a sparsely populated agricultural outpost of Guayaquil before the completion of the national railway in 1908, which connected Durán to Ecuador’s capital, Quito. Durán, which sits across the Guayas River from Guayaquil, was the perfect staging area for the storage and export of goods. Resid

articles.title: Durán, Ecuador’s New Crime Capital
articles.description: Durán has undergone a dramatic transformation from a thriving commercial center to a violent hub of criminal activity. This shift reflects broader challenges facing Ecuador and offers critical insights into the complex interplay of social, economic, and criminal factors shaping security in the count

articles.title: Dealing with the Devil: How Latin Kings in Ecuador Sought Peace, Waged Crime
articles.description: Diablo was a national leader of the Latin Kings in Guayaquil. The gang, whose roots stretched back seventy years to Chicago’s Latino neighborhoods, had been in Ecuador for two decades. While they had initially built a reputation for organizing music concerts and cultural events, they’d more recently

articles.title: Uruguay Corruption in Spotlight After Ex-Official’s Release From Prison
articles.description: Alejandro Astesiano, former head of security for ex-President of Uruguay Luis Lacalle Pou, was released from prison on September 20, two and a half years early, after serving time on organized crime charges. But as he starts a new chapter, Uruguay continues to struggle with questions about criminal

articles.title: Security Forces in Venezuela Extort Detainees Amid Political Repression
articles.description: Following the July 28 presidential elections, the Venezuelan government has carried out a massive wave of arrests. This has been accompanied by extortion, with capitalizing security officials promising not to arrest people or to release them from detention centers in exchange for money.

articles.title: Ecuador Gangs Target Prison Chiefs to Regain Control
articles.description: On the night of September 12, armed men fatally shot María Daniela Icaza, director of the Litoral Penitentiary in eastern Guayaquil, the country’s largest men’s prison and the site of some of the most harrowing massacres that have shaken Ecuador in recent years. The murder appears to be the latest a

articles.title: Central America Primed for Coca Expansion, Study Finds
articles.description: Nearly half of northern Central America has the ideal agricultural conditions for growing coca, the raw material for cocaine, according to a new study that warns of the potential spread of the illicit crop across the region.

articles.title: Narco-Subs Sail the Caribbean
articles.description: The Caribbean, historically a key region for drug trafficking, has once again become the center of attention with the seizure of a narco-submarine, signaling the use of these types of vessels is potentially becoming a more common tactic for drug shipments in this smuggling route.

articles.title: ELN Attack Destroys Chances for Peace With Strengthening Colombian Guerrilla Group
articles.description: Colombia’s government has ended peace talks with the country’s largest remaining guerrilla group after a deadly attack on a military base, effectively extinguishing the prospects for a deal with the group and leaving it stronger than when the negotiations started.

articles.title: Honduras Misses Reform Opportunities Amid Drug Scandal Turmoil
articles.description: Two weeks after InSight Crime released a video that showed the Honduran president’s brother-in-law negotiating with drug traffickers, the government has avoided proposing significant reforms in favor of portraying the publication of the video as a political plot.

articles.title: CJNG’s Fuel Theft Empire in the Crosshairs of US Treasury Sanctions
articles.description: The U.S. Department of the Treasury has sanctioned 26 Mexican companies involved in fuel theft, signaling the growing importance of this alternative criminal economy in the finances of major organized crime groups.

articles.title: Cauca’s Pacific Coast Sees Surge in Violence as Conflict Spreads
articles.description:
A new wave of violence in southwestern Colombia signals a worrying spread of conflict into previously calm territories.  
 On September 10, a powerful explosion hit the Pacific coastal town of Timbiquí, in Cauca, damaging more than 100 homes and injuring eight marines.

articles.title: Vitel’Homme Innocent
articles.description: Vitel’Homme Innocent is the leader of Kraze Baryè, one of Haiti’s largest gangs. Based in Port-au-Prince, the gang shot to prominence in 2021 after collaborating with 400 Mawozo, an allied gang, to kidnap 17 Christian missionaries, including five children. Kraze Baryè translates roughly to “Breaking

articles.title: Prediction: Rising Homicides in Trinidad & Tobago Amid Gang Violence
articles.description: Gang violence in Trinidad and Tobago has increased, and without significant policy change, predictive models suggest the trend will worsen. The twin-island nation is on pace to surpass the record 605 murders reported in 2022.

articles.title: Guy Philippe
articles.description: Guy Philippe is a convicted money launderer, former police commander, and politician, who helped lead the 2004 coup against Haiti’s then-president Jean-Bertrand Aristide. After serving a six-year prison sentence in the United States, authorities repatriated Philippe to Haiti in November 2023. These

articles.title: Inside the Battle for Rio de Janeiro: Red Command Versus Militias
articles.description: In Rio, the balance of power may be shifting. Fighting intensified in 2022 when the Red Command, a notorious gang with roots in the city’s prison system, has increased its efforts to reclaim territory from the militias, a network of paramilitary-style organizations that has held sway over many

articles.title: How Ecuador’s Criminals Are Taking Advantage of Private Security Firms
articles.description: Private security companies have surged in Ecuador as crime has intensified, but mounting evidence of criminal infiltration in the industry suggests that it may be servicing criminals  as much as the private sector and the government.

articles.title: Divisions Over Peace Talks Spark Surge in Armed Activity in Huila, Colombia
articles.description: Criminal activity has escalated in a key transit corridor in southern Colombia, driven by divisions between ex-FARC factions in the area, illustrating how government peace talks appear to be roiling relations among criminal factions.

articles.title: Honduras Profile
articles.description:
Honduras, long one of the poorest countries in Latin America, is now also among the most violent and crime-ridden. The violence is carried out by local drug trafficking groups, gangs, corrupt security forces and transnational criminal organizations mainly from Mexico and Colombia. 
 In recent years,

articles.title: Ecuador’s Kidnapping Epidemic, Told by a Survivor
articles.description:
Express kidnappings are classified as “common crimes” in Ecuador, and get scant attention compared to the transnational drug trafficking that has transformed the country into one of the region’s most violent in less than a decade. 
 But crimes like kidnapping and extortion have climbed at alarming r

articles.title: Increased Foreign Presence Around Guyana Is Helping Net More Cocaine
articles.description:
Guyanese authorities recently discovered 4.4 tons of cocaine with help from the US’s DEA. 
 The enormous seizure highlights the Guyana authorities’ dependence on foreign assistance in detecting the large quantities of cocaine that pass through the country, which is a major transit hub for the drug.

articles.title: Why Venezuela’s Repression of Tren del Llano Could Feed Anti-Regime Protests
articles.description:
Gang threats against the Venezuelan regime over July’s election results handed the government an opportunity to discredit the opposition and increase military activity.    
 But the move to crack down on the Tren del Llano gang could backfire by boosting anti-government sentiment and insecurity.

articles.title: Cachiros
articles.description: The Cachiros were one of Honduras’ largest transport groups, with a net worth close to $1 billion. Made up of a family of former cattle rustlers, the organization became a major player in the movement of cocaine between Colombian and Mexican organizations.

articles.title: Narco Video Shows Traffickers Discussing Bribes With Honduras President’s Brother-in-Law
articles.description: InSight Crime obtained a video in which some of Honduras’ top drug traffickers met with a member of the first family and offered to give over half a million dollars to help Honduras’ ruling party and an unsuccessful run for office by now-President Xiomara Castro. The video incriminates Carlos Zelaya

articles.title: Honduras Just Killed Its Best Drug War Weapon
articles.description:
The Honduran government announced a plan to end its extradition agreement with the United States in a move that will scupper the country’s ability to tackle drug trafficking and hold crime bosses to account.  
 The move for Honduras, which is a major cocaine transit hub in the region, could be aimed

articles.title: A 3-Ton Cocaine Seizure in Venezuela Highlights Asia Trafficking Routes
articles.description:
The seizure of a three-ton cocaine shipment at Venezuela’s main airport highlights the role of travel infrastructure in drug trafficking and exposes an unusual drug trafficking route. 
 But signals that the cocaine was going to be sent to Afghanistan could be a cover for other lucrative drug t

articles.title: Zetas
articles.description: Beginning as a group of deserters from an elite unit of the armed forces at the service of the Gulf Cartel, the Zetas would go on to become one of the most powerful and feared cartels in Mexico before infighting and the loss of leaders started the organization’s decline.

articles.title: Gulf Cartel OG Who Created Mexico’s Zetas Released From US Prison
articles.description:
The United States released Osiel Cárdenas Guillén from prison after more than 20 years behind bars. 
 The former Gulf Cartel leader, who walked free today, created the Zetas and ushered in the hyper-violent organized crime wars that have marked Mexico since the turn of the century.

articles.title: Osiel Cárdenas Guillén
articles.description: Osiel Cárdenas Guillén is the former leader of the Gulf Cartel (Cartel del Golfo – CDG). At the height of his power, he was one of Mexico’s most powerful crime bosses. Cárdenas created the Zetas, which he used as an armed wing before the group outgrew its master and went its own way, ushering in an

articles.title: Inside Argentina’s Biggest Wildlife Trafficking Bust
articles.description: Caza y Safaris Argentina ran tourist packages that illegally hunted animals in Argentina. It made hundreds of thousands of dollars of illegal profits for every tour it sold, and was just shut down by the government in what could be the biggest wildlife trafficking crime ever in Argentina.

articles.title: Arrests Shed Light on Abuse of Women in Guatemala’s Prisons
articles.description:
Guatemalan authorities have made multiple busts of prison guards involved in sexual exploitation schemes that victimize women behind bars.  
 The move shows rare government action on a problem that often remains hidden, and highlights the challenge of battling corruption in the penitentiary system.

articles.title: InSight Crime’s Sinaloa Coverage: From Culiacán to the Global Press
articles.description: With the dust still settling after the arrest of Sinaloa Cartel leader Ismael “El Mayo” Zambada, InSight Crime investigators Victoria Dittmar and Mike LaSusa arrived in Culiacán, state capital of […]

articles.title: Warring Criminal Groups Are Targeting Civilians in Chiapas, Mexico
articles.description:
An intensification of violence in the southern Mexican border state of Chiapas is pushing the number of displaced and missing people to new highs, data accessed by InSight Crime shows. 
 Warring criminal groups are forcibly pulling in civilians, and many residents are resorting to fleeing across the

articles.title: 4 Ways AI Is Shaping Organized Crime in Latin America
articles.description:
With access to artificial intelligence increasing rapidly, some of Latin America’s organized crime groups have begun wielding it for criminal gain, and law enforcement is struggling to keep up.   
 Deep fakes, frauds, phishing and malware are four of the ways  criminal syndicates are leveragin

articles.title: *Summer InSights 2024
articles.description: Welcome to Summer InSights, our seasonal special where we share a selection of some of our most engaging publications to keep you entertained as well as informed about Latin America’s underworld.

articles.title: Who Is Behind the Murder of a Powerful Colombian Emerald Dealer?
articles.description:
Colombia’s emerald trade, renowned for the exceptional quality of its gems as well as its relentless violence, has suffered another blow to its reputation with the professional assassination of a top dealer.  
 The killing has sparked speculation over which criminal powers beyond the jewel business

articles.title: Ex-Intel Official Explains Chile’s Organized Crime Explosion
articles.description: Organized crime consultant and former intelligence official in Chile’s Carabineros police force Pablo Zeballos analyzes the growing strength of organized crime in Chile. The loss of faith in the country’s institutions, the influence of transnational criminal organizations, the weaknesses within its

articles.title: After Venezuela Elections, Criminal Groups Work For or Against the Regime
articles.description:
Armed groups including colectivos, the ELN and even the criminal gang Tren del Llano have waded into post-election violence following last month’s presidential vote.  
 Some are repressing protests to the results announced by the regime, others are going against the government. InSight Crime r

articles.title: A Cold War Is Raging Inside the Sinaloa Cartel Following El Mayo’s Capture
articles.description:
Following the capture of El Mayo, tension is growing between the Mayiza and the Chapitos factions of the Sinaloa Cartel — especially in the criminal hub of Sinaloa’s state capital Culiacán. 
 We traveled across Mexico, speaking with criminal actors and others to report on the brewing con

articles.title: Gulf Cartel
articles.description: The Gulf Cartel is among the oldest most powerful cartels in Mexico, but has lost territory in recent years.

articles.title: Cryptocurrency Money Laundering Is on the Rise in Brazil

articles.description:
The arrest of cabinet member Ligia Hernández in Guatemala marks an ominous escalation of a spurious legal crusade aimed at derailing the anti-corruption administration of President Bernardo Arévalo.

Controversy and conflicting narratives persist around the arrests of two top Sinaloa Cartel leaders in the United States last month.

articles.title: Technology Is Increasingly at the Heart of Criminal Operations in Brazil
articles.description: The recent dismantling of communication towers and illegal cellphone shops in Brazil points to the increasing use of technology by criminal groups. It also shows how victims can be targeted by aggressors both within and beyond the South American country’s borders.

articles.title: InSight Crime Provides Expertise on Venezuela’s Hybrid Criminal Governance

articles.title: Two Years On, Colombia’s ‘Total Peace’ Brings More Conflict
articles.description:

articles.description:
In a move that could potentially turn Colombia’s criminal landscape on its head, the government has announced it will open negotiations with the country’s most powerful drug trafficking organization, the AGC.

articles.title: US Customs Makes Historic Fentanyl Seizure
articles.description:
A massive seizure of the drug suggests that fentanyl production is thriving in Mexico, despite a supposed ban by certain criminal groups under massive US pressure.

articles.title: Colombia’s Total Peace Hangs by a Thread After Ceasefire With ELN Expires
articles.description:
Peace negotiations with Colombia’s last true guerrilla insurgency are on the verge of collapse after the August 3 expiration of a ceasefire between the government and the ELN.  
 The bilateral ceasefire, in effect for the past year, ended after the National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Na

articles.title: Ketamine Trafficking Rising in Chile’s Thriving Synthetic Drugs Market
articles.description: Record seizures of ketamine in Chile show how the drug is making its way into the country’s already extensive synthetic drug market.

articles.title: Leading Global Conversations on Organized Crime News in Latin America

articles.description: Growing rates of informal immigration in Latin America make the region increasingly vulnerable to human trafficking for organ removal.

articles.title: Costa Rica Considers Extradition to Counter Growing Judicial Corruption
articles.description: Costa Rican lawmakers have advanced a proposed constitutional amendment to allow the extradition of Costa Rican drug traffickers and terrorists wanted by foreign authorities, highlighting growing concern that organized crime is corrupting the judiciary’s ability to confront it.

articles.title: Colombia Profile
articles.description: For decades, Colombia has been the world’s leading cocaine producer. Numerous Colombian criminal groups have entered the drug trade through alliances with mafias worldwide.

articles.title: What Is Behind the Surge in Cocaine Seizures in the French Caribbean in 2024?
articles.description: Record-breaking cocaine seizures in the French Caribbean in 2024 appear to be the result of rampant cocaine manufacturing in Latin America, along with increased cooperation between French and Venezuelan authorities.

articles.title: Popular Liberation Army (Ejército Popular de Liberación – EPL)
articles.description: What is known today as the Popular Liberation Army (Ejército Popular de Liberación – EPL), or the Pelusos, is a dissident faction of the original EPL’s Libardo Mora Front that refused to demobilize with the rest of that guerrilla group in 1991. Its main area of operation is located in the Cata

articles.title: Predictions for Venezuela’s Post-Election Criminal Landscape
articles.description: Nicolas Maduro claimed another six years in power after a disputed election, which will result in another wave of migration and Venezuela cementing its position as a regional crime hub.

articles.title: Ismael Zambada García, alias ‘El Mayo’
articles.description: Ismael Zambada Garcia, alias “El Mayo,” is battlng the Chapitos for control of the Sinaloa Cartel.

articles.title: Sinaloa Cartel Leaders Arrested in Symbolic, but Hollow Victory for US
articles.description: In a stunning turn of events, two of the Sinaloa Cartel’s most-wanted drug traffickers are in custody in the United States, marking a historic moment for organized crime in Mexico but one that is unlikely to upend the flow of synthetic drugs across the border.

articles.title: How Maduro’s Criminal Regime Is Tilting the Venezuela Elections
articles.description: This Sunday’s presidential elections will define not only the democratic future of Venezuela but its criminal evolution, and President Nicolás Maduro has deployed illegal and repressive tools in a bid to cement his power for another six years.

articles.title: Pressure Grows for Kenyan Police Fighting Haiti’s Gangs
articles.description: The second contingent of 200 Kenyan police officers arrived in Haiti on July 16, joining 200 of their compatriots who landed on June 25. As these officers work to support Haiti’s embattled police force, concerns about their ability to thwart the country’s powerful street gangs are growing.

articles.title: As Government Pressure Mounts, Ecuador’s Gangs Strike Gold
articles.description: Informal mining and illegal gold exports have existed for decades in Ecuador. articles.title: Latin American Drug Traffickers Evade Authorities With Unmanned Vessels and Chemical Innovations
articles.description: Latin American drug networks are increasingly using unmanned vessels and employing new methods to chemically transform cocaine to move large drug shipments.

articles.title: How Mexico’s CJNG Controls Guadalajara’s Expanding Drug Market
articles.description: One of Mexico’s most powerful crime groups, the Jalisco Cartel New Generation (Cartel Jalisco Nueva Generación – CJNG), is employing forced disappearance and corruption in its efforts to build up a burgeoning local drug market on its home turf of Guadalajara.

articles.title: Is Venezuela’s Tren de Aragua Behind Surge in Chile Kidnappings?
articles.description: A recent surge in kidnappings in Chile has coincided with Tren de Aragua, Venezuela’s most brutal prison gang, putting down roots.

articles.title: What’s Behind Falling Cocaine Seizures in the Netherlands and Belgium?
articles.description: Europe’s two main entry points for cocaine have reported a drastic drop in seizures at maritime ports during the first half of 2024, but this is likely indicative not of reduced drug flows from Latin America but rather the adaptability of transnational organized crime.

articles.title: Convictions in Ecuador Political Assassination, But No Clarity
articles.description: Last week a judge in Ecuador sentenced five people convicted in the high-profile 2023 murder of presidential candidate Fernando Villavicencio, but there is still no clarity as to who ordered the killing and why.

articles.title: Challenges Remain Despite Drop in Cattle Rustling in Colombia
articles.description:
A big drop in cattle rustling in Colombia appears to be related to the ever-smaller criminal networks involved in this criminal market.  
 However, the crime is underreported and authorities are far from eradicating it completely.

articles.title: Salvatore Mancuso
articles.description: Salvatore Mancuso, alias “El Mono” or “Santander Lozada,” is a former Colombian paramilitary commander who was part of the Peasant Self Defence Forces of Córdoba and Urabá (Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Urabá – ACCU) and the United Self-Defence Forces of Colombia (Autodefensas Unidas de

articles.title: Héctor Rusthenford Guerrero Flores, alias ‘Niño Guerrero’
articles.description: Hector Rusthenford Guerrero Flores, alias “Niño” Guerrero,” is the main leader of Tren de Aragua, the most important criminal group in Venezuela.

articles.title: Colombia’s Historic Drop in Deforestation Could Be Linked to Criminal Groups
articles.description:
Deforestation in Colombia has fallen to its lowest levels in 23 years, suggesting the government’s fight against environmental crime has yielded results.  
 But criminal groups, and the changing dynamics between them, also play a role in the decrease in destruction.

articles.title: US Labels Tren de Aragua a Transnational Criminal Threat
articles.description:
The recent US designation of the Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua as a transnational criminal organization puts the group on par with major security threats in Latin America.  
 It also presents a new challenge to the gang’s so-far unstoppable regional expansion.

articles.title: InSight Crime Will Be Honored by the Maria Moors Cabot Prize
articles.description: The prestigious recognition given by the Columbia Journalism School honors journalists and news organizations for career excellence and coverage of the Western Hemisphere that furthers inter-American understanding. We are honored to be among its many illustrious recipients.

articles.title: Mexico’s Meth Producers Are Making Their Own Precursors, Government Says

articles.title: The Mafias Behind Sand Trafficking in Latin America
articles.description:
Criminal mafias are making massive profits from the illegal extraction and sale of sand for construction purposes.

articles.description: Alejandra is one of many victims of “gota a gota” loans in Venezuela, a criminal activity that consists of offering short-term cash credit at high interest rates. When people are unable to pay, criminals resort to violence to intimidate them.

articles.description: Brazilian cyber gangs are increasingly stealing data and holding it for ransom. Law enforcement is struggling to keep up with the explosion of cyber crimes in the country, which are hitting record highs.

articles.description:

articles.title: Tren del Llano
articles.description: Tren del Llano (Train of the Plain) is one of the first so-called “megabandas” or criminal gangs to develop in Venezuela.

articles.title: In Battle to Slow Precursor Chemicals in Mexico, Public-Private Partnerships Are a Path Not Taken
articles.description: Public-Private Partnerships have worked around the world to fight organized crime. Similar efforts to mitigate the impact of synthetic drugs have yet to have impact.

articles.title: Chile Ups Efforts Against ‘Narco’ Mausoleums and Funerals

articles.title: From Singing in Court to Alleged International Cocaine Deals: The Fall of Brazil’s Nelma Kodama

articles.title: Lime Crisis in Mexico as Cartels Target Farmers

articles.title: Brazil Tries to Reclaim Rio’s Favelas – Ad Infinitum

What’s behind the deaths of the Segunda Marquetalia’s leaders? A war on drugs, internal divisions or the Colombian

articles.description: Lacking government support and effective oversight, drug rehab centers in Mexico are being exploited by organized crime groups, which are taking advantage of their anarchical state to disappear people and recruit lookouts, hitmen and drug dealers.

articles.description: The number of human trafficking victims in Mexico is growing, as traffickers target vulnerable people hit hard by the COVID-19 pandemic.

articles.description: Mexico’s process of criminal fragmentation has been a slow burn. Many of the country’s mightiest criminal groups have been unable to stay united, either due to internal strife, incursions from rivals or the arrests of key leaders.

articles.description: The alleged exporter of a shipment of 26 tons of illegal shark fins from Ecuador to Hong Kong has received a fine of a few thousand dollars, showing how people and companies involved in the trade escape serious penalties.

articles.title: Ex-Lieutenant Allegedly Recruits Venezuelan Soldiers for Gang Attack on Illegal Mine
articles.description: A former army lieutenant allegedly recruited Venezuelan soldiers to provide firepower for the Tren de Guayana gang in a bid to take over gold mining operations in southern Venezuela, revealing the ever more complex web of collaboration between the military and criminal gangs.

articles.title: Vigilante Death Squad Sends Bloody Message in Brazil-Paraguay Border Region
articles.description: A shadowy vigilante death squad operating on the Brazil-Paraguay border is killing supposed thieves and then leaving notes next to their mutilated bodies — a macabre message even for a region steeped in drug violence.

articles.title: Are El Koki and His Gang Members No Longer Safe in Venezuela?
articles.description: Police in Venezuela have killed a notorious gang leader while others are reportedly hiding in Colombia, raising questions about the state of one of Caracas’ most powerful criminal structures and its leader, El Koki.

articles.title: Weapons Traffickers Target Chile’s Port of Iquique
articles.description: After a seizure of disassembled guns found in vehicles shipped from the United States to Chile, a prosecutor warned that the South American country – not typically a hub for illegal firearms – had seen an uptick in weapons trafficking through its northern port.

articles.title: Documents Reveal Attempts to Curb Guatemala’s Anti-Corruption Unit
articles.description: Before the firing of Juan Francisco Sandoval, Guatemala’s top anti-corruption prosecutor, Attorney General Consuelo Porras pressured him to curtail investigations while his unit probed accusations of corruption in Porras’ office.

articles.title: El Salvador Mayors Cash in on Migrant Smuggling
articles.description: In two separate cases in El Salvador, mayors are accused of heading migrant smuggling rings, a crime that, like drug trafficking, local officials in border regions are well positioned to […]

articles.title: Extermination Sites: The New Depths of Mexico’s Disappearance Crisis
articles.description: Families combing for clues about their vanished loved ones at recently discovered extermination sites in northern Mexico have turned to local cartel leaders for help – revealing their desperation to find any trace of the disappeared amid masses of incinerated bones.

articles.title: 3 Takeaways from Jovenel Moïse’s Murder Investigation in Haiti
articles.description: Nearly a month after the midnight assassination of former Haiti President Jovenel Moïse, many of the details about the murder plot remain murky despite scores of arrests.

articles.title: A Blend of Family and Politics in Guatemala’s Cocaine Trade
articles.description: An anti-narcotics operation in western Guatemala has shed further light on how families involved in local politics can play a pivotal role in the regional cocaine trade.

articles.title: How Dominican Gangs Became Major Security Threat in Spain
articles.description: Spiraling violence around Madrid is being blamed on the fracturing and spread of Dominican gangs, which have become Spain’s primary urban security threat.

articles.title: Guns in the Gulf: Mexico’s Navy Called to Protect Against Pirates
articles.description: After a spate of attacks on oil platforms in the Gulf of Mexico this year, the shipping industry is demanding protection, including a stronger presence from the Mexican Navy.

articles.title: How Many Creepy-Crawlies Can a Trafficker Hide in His Luggage?
articles.description: There is one crucial quality for any successful wildlife trafficker: tenacity. And Kirill Kravchenko had it in spades.

articles.title: Colombia’s Cocaine Keeps On Reaching New Heights: UNODC Report
articles.description: While the amount of coca crops in Colombia has dipped for the third year, the country’s cocaine production continues to reach record highs, according to new figures from the United Nations.

articles.title: Is Colombia’s Military Deployment Playing into FARC Dissidents’ Hands?
articles.description: Colombia’s announcement of a large-scale military deployment to Norte de Santander, one of its most complex criminal hotspots, has thrust a relatively small-scale criminal actor, the 33rd Front, to national relevance.

articles.title: Brazilian Authorities Clamp Down on Cattle Theft
articles.description: A number of arrests made to tackle a sophisticated cattle rustling operation in Brazil have suggested authorities are stepping up efforts to target the crime.

articles.title: Drug Gangs, Thieves Team Up for Violent Cargo Robberies in Rio
articles.description: A cargo-theft ring in Rio de Janeiro partnered with a drug gang to steal millions of dollars’ worth of goods, showing how criminal actors in Brazil come together to commit big heists.

articles.title: Fishing Cooperatives Used to Mask Drug Shipments into Mexico
articles.description: Mexico’s largest criminal groups are outsourcing the retrieval of cocaine shipments to smaller groups posing as fishing cooperatives, providing another example of how maritime infrastructure is subverted by the drug trade.

articles.title: Unraveling the Web of Elites Connected to Organized Crime
articles.description: InSight Crime published Elites and Organized Crime in Nicaragua, a deep dive into the relationships between criminal actors and elites in that Central American nation.

articles.title: Migrants Disappearing, Dying as US-Mexico Border Remains Closed
articles.description: Reports of migrants dying and disappearing in the US-Mexico borderlands are becoming increasingly common, propelled in part by a restrictive border policy pushing people to cross through more remote and treacherous routes.

articles.title: Argentina Spares Mother Who Served as Drug Mule to Save Son
articles.description: Drug charges against a woman in Argentina were dropped after prosecutors learned that she had agreed to be a drug mule to pay for her son’s surgery, in a case that shows prosecutors considered how women can be exploited by traffickers.

articles.title: Bala na Cara
articles.description: Bala na Cara (BNC) is a prominent criminal group in Brazil’s southern state of Rio Grande do Sul.

articles.title: Self-Defense Group Emerges After Criminal Attacks in Chiapas, Mexico
articles.description: Amid government inaction, Indigenous communities in the highlands of Mexico’s southern state of Chiapas are throwing their support behind a self-defense group that formed to fend off attacks by organized crime groups on their communities.

articles.title: Despite Outrage, Guatemala Continues to Bulldoze Anti-Corruption Edifice
articles.description: The dismissal of Juan Francisco Sandoval, Guatemala’s leading anti-corruption prosecutor who has since fled the country, has drawn condemnation at home and abroad.

articles.title: Religious Statuettes Branded as El Koki for Sale in Caracas
articles.description: Salesmen in Caracas have come up with an enterprising response to the Venezuelan capital’s descent into criminal warfare: selling devotional statuettes dressed like members of El Koki’s gang.

articles.title: FARC Dissidents Want Old Land Back in Colombia’s Caquetá and Meta
articles.description: Once belonging to the demobilized FARC, certain criminal groups seek to reconquer surrendered assets, driving a wave of violence in southern and central Colombia.

articles.description: After peddling fake tests and vaccines for the coronavirus, criminals are now selling counterfeit vaccination certificates in Mexico City, allowing their customers to lie about their vaccine status and travel abroad.

articles.title: Bolivia Seemingly Powerless to Stop Rampant Vehicle Smuggling
articles.description: A recent clash between soldiers and smugglers has pointed to how Bolivia’s trade in contraband has reached a flashpoint.

articles.title: Paraguay Grapples With Ever Larger Seizures of Marijuana
articles.description: A string of large marijuana seizures in Paraguay’s eastern border departments underscores how the region continues to be a key source for illegal marijuana trafficked to Brazil.

articles.title: Fentanyl and 2CB, Worrying New Cocktail on Colombia’s Party Circuit
articles.description: A synthetic drug popular in Colombia’s party scene was recently found to be laced with the powerful synthetic opioid fentanyl, creating a deadly mix that has not been previously reported in Latin America.

articles.title: InSight Crime’s Greater Focus on US-Mexico Border
articles.description: InSight Crime has decided to turn many of its investigative resources towards understanding and chronicling the criminal dynamics along the US-Mexico border.

articles.title: Human Smugglers Profit From Despair of Haitian Migrants in Brazil
articles.description: Government officials in Brazil say smugglers are moving Haitian migrants across the country’s border with Peru, as many of them take a first step in the perilous journey north toward the United States.

articles.description: The United States saw a record toll in drug overdose deaths last year, driven in part by two powerful synthetic drugs mass-produced in Mexico and then smuggled over the border.

articles.title: Alejandro ‘Omar’ Treviño Morales, alias ‘Z42’

articles.description: Miguel Angel Treviño, alias “Z40,” led the feared Zetas in Mexico before his capture in 2013.

articles.description: One of Colombia’s foremost drug traffickers and money launderers has been assassinated in Bogotá, leaving important unanswered questions about his own criminal past and who might have been after him.

articles.description: For some two decades, Luis Agustin Caicedo Velandia, alias “Don Lucho,” is alleged to have played a significant role in trafficking drugs out of Colombia’s prized Eastern Plains region.

articles.title: Nicaragua Elites and Organized Crime: Ted Hayman, the Cocaine Fisherman
articles.description: Nicaraguan Fisherman Ted Hayman Forbes and his drug trafficking network operated from Panama, Costa Rica, Nicaragua and the Colombian island of San Andres in the Caribbean. His capture and sentencing put an end to a criminal career, which local and Nicaraguan elites had aided and abetted for years.

articles.title: Nicaragua Elites and Organized Crime: Introduction
articles.description: In the process of expanding their influence, criminal groups often develop close ties with elites in an effort to gain protection and access to better resources. This facet of transnational organized crime, and the threats it poses to the security and economies of today’s societies, is an issue that

articles.title: Is Organized Crime Tied to Haiti President’s Assassination?
articles.description: A convicted cocaine trafficker is among the suspects that authorities in Haiti are pursuing in connection to the middle-of-the-night murder of former President Jovenel Moïse, raising questions about whether organized […]

articles.title: Nicaragua Elites and Organized Crime: The Case of Henry Fariñas
articles.description: The murder of a prominent folk singer in Guatemala thrust a Nicaraguan nightclub owner into the spotlight and revealed an illicit drug trafficking network that stretched from Colombia to Mexico.

articles.title: 5 Reasons For Record Deforestation in Colombia

articles.description: Killings connected to a number of military police units in São Paulo fell to zero in June 2021 due to a simple innovation: body cameras.

articles.title: Is Venezuela Getting Serious About Unseating El Koki?

articles.description: Migrants from Venezuela’s Warao Indigenous community who have been forced to flee to Guyana find themselves forcibly recruited to work as illegal miners in their new country.

articles.description: Two days on from the nighttime assassination of Haiti President Jovenel Moïse in Port-au-Prince, competing theories have failed to provide any real motive for the high-profile hit.

articles.description: With Memo Fantasma’s arrest, InSight Crime has proven that our investigations can and will uncover major criminal threats in the Americas.

articles.description: A series of photos taken in a Mexican town, that has been the focal point of major criminal engagements this year, reportedly shows members of the CJNG with faces uncovered for the first time.

articles.description: A US investigation has shed light on the alleged participation of Guatemalan soldiers in a cocaine smuggling network linked to Mexico’s Sinaloa Cartel, a reminder of the pivotal role the armed forces can play in the international drug trade.

articles.description: Newly leaked audio recordings have sparked a political firestorm in Brazil – linking President Jair Bolsonaro to an extortion scheme where political staffers had to give up part of their salaries in order to keep their jobs, a practice known as “rachadinha.” InSight Crime looks bac

articles.title: Haiti President Assassinated in Port-au-Prince
articles.description: Armed gunmen have murdered Haiti President Jovenel Moïse and severely wounded First Lady Martine Moïse in a coordinated attack on their home, marking the most high-profile act of violence to hit the Caribbean nation amid significant unrest recently.

articles.description: Multiple sources from the United States and El Salvador say the recent decision to temporarily halt the extradition of several top MS13 leaders to face US charges may be related to an ongoing, unofficial pact between the gang and the Salvadoran government. However, the case, as well as the existence

articles.title: Fishermen Fight Off Pirate Attacks in Ecuador
articles.description: Fishermen in Ecuador have taken matters into their own hands to combat pirate attacks that have long plagued its coastal provinces.

articles.title: In the Eye of Venezuela’s Perfect Criminal Storm
articles.description: InSight Crime Project Manager James Bargent held a virtual seminar for postgraduate students and academics titled “Organized Crime, Conflict and Isolation: the Perfect Storm in Venezuela.”

articles.title: Organized Crime’s Influence on Gender-Based Violence

articles.description: In the absence of an adequate COVID-19 vaccination plan, criminal networks in Venezuela have seized upon ongoing mismanagement to steal and resell doses or sell fake vaccines on the black market.

articles.description: The owner of an armored transport company has been charged for his part in a transnational dirty gold network that moved the metal to the United States via Caribbean islands.

articles.title: Land Thieves Drove People From Their Homes in Rosario, Argentina

articles.title: Video: How Memo Fantasma Was Discovered

articles.title: Killings in Cali: What is to Blame for the Spike?
articles.description: Convulsed by nearly eight weeks of anti-government protests, the Colombian city of Cali has also experienced a terrible surge in murders.

articles.description: Guillermo León Acevedo Giraldo, alias “Memo Fantasma,” a former paramilitary leader and longtime drug trafficker who escaped prosecution for decades by living behind a façade of legitimate business dealings, was captured June 25 by Colombian authorities.

articles.description: El Coqui seemed to be comfortable. Caracas’ foremost gang boss had, for several years, dominated the sprawling neighborhood of Cota 905, a densely packed and poor part of western Caracas.

articles.description: The retirement of the ELN’s top commander and political leader may have sweeping implications for the guerrilla group as it seeks to maintain its favorable position in both Colombia and Venezuela.

articles.title: Conversation with Paraguay Judicial Operators on PCC
articles.description: InSight Crime Co-director Steven Dudley formed part of a panel attended by over 500 students, all of whom work in Paraguay’s judicial system.

articles.title: Hashish: New Player in Brazil Drug Markets
articles.description: In stopping a European sailboat, Brazilian authorities have made a record seizure of marijuana resin, also known as hashish, revealing both a new trans-Atlantic drug route and the ongoing diversification within Brazil’s booming marijuana markets.

articles.title: Eliecer Erlinto Chamorro, alias ‘Antonio Garcia’
articles.description: Eliecer Erlinto Chamorro, alias “  Antonio Garcia  ,” is the number two commander in Colombias National Liberation Army (  ELN  ) rebel group.

articles.title: ‘Blue’: The Story of MS13’s First ‘Terrorist’
articles.description: On the eve of the extradition of top leaders of El Salvador’s feared Mara Salvatrucha (MS13) gang to the United States on terrorism charges, InSight Crime recounts the story of the first MS13 member designated as a terrorist and the process that led the United States to make that historic decision.

articles.title: Fishing Village Caught in Crosshairs of Ecuador Drug Gangs
articles.description: In Ecuador’s coastal town of Posorja, boats are being set on fire and people are being killed, as this fishing community – a key cocaine transit hub – becomes entangled in drug gang warfare.

articles.title: ICU Beds Trafficked in Peru Amidst COVID-19 Spike
articles.description: As Peru reels from having the worst COVID-19 caseload in the world, criminals are extorting the families of patients in exchange for having access to the few ICU beds that remain.

articles.title: High-Profile Corruption Probe Tests Costa Rica’s Justice System
articles.description: At a time when elites are undermining anti-graft efforts across Central America, prosecutors in Costa Rica have launched a far-reaching investigation into a bribery scheme between government officials and construction executives, in a key test for the country’s justice system.

articles.title: Cartel War or General Chaos: Behind the Killing Spree in Reynosa, Mexico
articles.description: Though the motive for the rampage in the northern Mexican border city of Reynosa that left scores of civilians dead remains unexplained, security experts point to three possible scenarios: a war within the Gulf Cartel, a political settling of scores, or a mere desire to sow chaos.

articles.description: A massacre between the Barrio 18 and MS13 gangs in Honduras’ most notorious prison raises questions on whether the military is having any impact since taking over responsibility for prison security in December 2019.

articles.title: How Mexico’s Drug Trade Has Evolved Over a Century
articles.description: Misconceptions surrounding Mexico’s drug trade have long been replicated in popular culture and public discourses.

articles.title: Eat Them or Extradite Them – What to Do With Pablo Escobar’s Hippos?
articles.description: Colombia’s hippos are a well-documented problem. Growing astonishingly fast in number, potentially devastating to the local ecosystem and perhaps lethal to humans, plenty of solutions have been proposed.

articles.title: Mozambique Becoming Southern Africa’s Cocaine Platform
articles.description: Ongoing seizures of cocaine trafficked from Brazil to Mozambique continue to highlight the robust drug route between the two former Portuguese colonies, as well as a lesser-known transit region for Europe-bound cocaine.

articles.title: Colombia Drug Trafficking Money Laundered Through Modified Gold
articles.description: A criminal group in Colombia is turning dirty money into adulterated gold, in the latest addition to a long list of laundering schemes using the precious metal.

articles.title: Killing of Brazil’s Top Militia Leader Raises More Questions Than it Answers
articles.description: Rio de Janeiro’s foremost militia leader has been gunned down by police, potentially shattering the belief that militias in the city were protected due to connections with authorities. But was this a lone action or the start of a real reckoning?

articles.title: Combating Environmental Crime in Colombia
articles.description: InSight Crime presented findings from an investigation into the main criminal activities fueling environmental destruction in Colombia.

articles.title: Cuba Quietly Admits to Wildlife Trafficking Problem
articles.description: Though Cuba’s illegal animal trade rarely makes headlines, its government has quietly raised alarms with a new wildlife trafficking law.

articles.title: Cocaine, Crack, Meth, MDMA – Europe Drugs Report Paints Grim Picture
articles.description: A recent report on drug trafficking and consumption in Europe highlights the acceleration of existing trends and the emergence of new ones, both of which impact Latin America’s criminal landscape.

articles.title: Haiti’s Mighty G9 Gang Alliance Tries to Keep it Together
articles.description: An alliance of Haitian gangs, known as G9, may be at risk as two of its founding members have fought bitterly in Port-au-Prince in recent weeks, threatening to bring further chaos to the city’s poorest neighborhoods.

articles.title: A Black Cap and A Photo – How John 40 Shook Up FARC Dissidents
articles.description: A former FARC leader and veteran drug trafficker has sided with ex-commander Iván Márquez, a move that could prove crucial to the future of dissident FARC criminal groups in Colombia and Venezuela.

articles.title: Mexico Authorities on Alert After Millions of Rounds of Ammunition Stolen
articles.description: Over seven million rounds of ammunition were stolen by an unknown armed group in central Mexico this week, a record-breaking heist that could escalate fighting in one of the country’s most violent areas.

articles.title: 3 Security Challenges Facing Peru’s Incoming President
articles.description: In arguably the most polarizing election Peru has seen in its history, voters have selected, by a razor-thin margin, a leftist rural school teacher over the daughter of a former dictator to be the Andean nation’s next president, a position that will come with a series of pressing security issues.

articles.title: Atlantic Entrance to Panama Canal Awash with Cocaine
articles.description: Panama’s northern province of Colón, sitting at the Atlantic entrance to the Panama Canal, is seeing a truly staggering increase in drug seizures, raising questions about its role in the Central American cocaine pipeline.

articles.title: Gatonet – The Illegal TV Connections Bankrolling Militias in Rio
articles.description: By undercutting legal streaming apps, militia groups are providing an illegal television service to millions of households in Brazil, earning them a fortune and bankrolling other criminal activities.

articles.title: Could Militia-Red Command Feud Explain Manaus Violence in Brazil?
articles.description: Manaus, the economic heart of Brazil’s Amazon, has come under siege from the Red Command gang after the killing of a top drug leader. But these attacks may be the latest chapter in an escalating nationwide criminal feud.

articles.title: Why Mexico’s National Guard Remains Vastly Unqualified
articles.description: Over 90 percent of active personnel in Mexico’s National Guard remain uncertified two years after the police body’s creation, marking the failure of a signature goal of Mexican President Andrés Manuel López Obrador, who stood up the new force.

articles.title: Land Restitution: A Dangerous Job in Colombia
articles.description: Repeated attacks on teams recovering stolen lands in Colombia highlight the risks involved in restitution, a little-known process that is nevertheless critical to the country’s peace agreement.

articles.title: Collaborating on Citizen Security Initiatives
articles.description: Co-director Steven Dudley worked with Chemonics, a DC-based development firm, to analyze the organization’s citizen security programs in Mexico.

articles.title: Florida’s Gun Traffickers Supplying Brazil’s Largest Gang
articles.description: Florida has been unable to shake its reputation as a go-to destination for Latin American criminals to secure guns and ammunition, with Brazil’s most powerful gang the latest recipient.

articles.title: Coup de Grâce for El Salvador’s Anti-Corruption Commission
articles.description: The announcement of the end of El Salvador’s anti-graft commission, which had been backed by the Organization of American States, could be the nail in the coffin for international investigative bodies in the region.

articles.title: Chinese Fishing Fleet Still Baits Argentina
articles.description: China’s distant-water fishing fleet is once again engaging in suspicious and potentially illegal fishing near Argentinean waters, demonstrating the limits of both regional and Chinese government attempts to rope in the country’s industry.

articles.title: Nicaragua Government Weaponizes Criminal Charges to Block Opposition
articles.description: With his leading opponent under house arrest for money laundering charges, Nicaragua President Daniel Ortega appears to be weaponizing the justice system to block his competition before upcoming presidential elections.

articles.title: Loophole Spurs Large-Scale Land Grabs in Brazil
articles.description: A government registry in Brazil has been used to designate millions of  hectares of Amazon forest as rural lands, in a process that critics say fuels state-sanctioned land grabs and deforestation by criminal actors.

articles.description: The Colombian government has, rather optimistically, announced the demise of one of the country’s more aggressive criminal groups, in a region that has seen the constant recycling of criminal actors since the days of the Medellin Cartel.

articles.title: Displacements in Mexico Increase With Ongoing Violence
articles.description: The number of displaced people within Mexico has increased for the first time in three years, indicating that the country’s powerful criminal groups have stepped up the type of public violence that harms citizen security.

articles.title: Barbados at Crossroads with Rising Homicide Rate
articles.description: With its homicide rates higher than ever in 2019 and 2020, Barbados is now confronting a difficult question: will it be able to bring the violence down or will it catch up with some of its Caribbean neighbors?

articles.title: Timber Mafias at Ecuador’s Borders Cash in on Balsa Boom
articles.description: Timber mafias operating along Ecuador’s borders with Peru and Colombia have been profiting from thriving demand for balsa wood.

articles.title: InSight Crime Deepens Its Connections with Universities
articles.description: A partnership with the University for Peace will complement InSight Crime’s research methodology and expertise on Costa Rica.

articles.title: With Support from USAID, InSight Crime Will Investigate Organized Crime in Haiti
articles.description: The project will seek to map out Haiti’s principal criminal economies, profile the specific groups and actors, and detail their links to elements of the state.

articles.description: The Catholic Church has played a central role in negotiating peace between Mexican drug cartels. But religious leaders in Michoacán are now starting to help residents flee the area – a shift in approach that suggests the violence is reaching new levels.

articles.title: No Sloth Selfies – The Rush to Protect Latin America’s Slowest Mammal

articles.description: While contraband has boomed at the Argentina-Brazil border during the COVID-19 pandemic, wine seems to have been in particularly high demand, with one high-profile murder being linked to its smuggling.

articles.title: ‘Cartel of the Robe’ Reveals High-Level Corruption in Colombia’s Courts

articles.description: The central Colombian department of Antioquia has been the scene of four massacres over the first two months of 2021, as the Urabeños trafficking group aims to expand both its routes to the Pacific coast and local drug markets.

articles.description: Criminal dynamics in Brazil, particularly its drug trafficking routes, have been shaken up in recent years by the rapid expansion of the PCC.

articles.title: Prison Break Lays Bare Haiti Gang Leader’s Ties to Elites
articles.description: A gang leader killed after a deadly Haiti prison break may have been sprung intentionally — raising further concerns about toxic relationships between gangs and political elites in the run-up […]

articles.title: Stay Frosty: Further Innovations in Drug Trafficking
articles.description: Corn flakes “frosted” in cocaine and fentanyl pills hidden inside toys might make for amusing headlines, but these recent discoveries demonstrate how commonplace the smuggling of drugs inside consumer goods has become.

articles.title: Parrots, Toucans and Monkeys Delivered Across Mexico

articles.description: The head of El Salvador’s financial regulatory agency has instructed banks not to close the accounts of suspected or formally accused money launderers, the latest in a long line of incidents that expose El Salvador’s hollow fight against graft.

articles.title: Lorenzanas
articles.description: The Lorenzanas are a traditional contraband family, who, over time, became enmeshed in the lucrative drug trade. The group’s criminal career ended in the early 2010s with the arrest and extradition of its top leaders.

articles.title: Migrants at Risk as Coronavirus Shutters Mexico Shelters
articles.description: The coronavirus pandemic has forced migrant shelters in Mexico to close or limit capacity, exacerbating an already precarious situation for migrants vulnerable to the predations of criminal groups.

articles.title: Allegations Against Honduras President Add to Narco-State Case
articles.description: With new accusations that Honduras President Juan Orlando Hernández directly brokered deals to protect traffickers in exchange for drug money, the president himself has emerged as a lynchpin in Honduras’ descent into a narco-state.

articles.title: Libya, North Africa Emerge As Cocaine Transit Hubs
articles.description: Within the span of a week, cocaine was discovered in two separate maritime cargo containers bound for Libya, a strong indication that both the north African country and broader region are becoming new cocaine transit routes to Europe and the Middle East.

articles.title: Rio Militias and Their Complex Empire of Legal Businesses
articles.description: As part of a mounting campaign going after criminal finances, police in Rio de Janeiro have asked courts to seize control of legal businesses used by militia groups to launder money.

articles.title: Counterfeit Money Trade Thrives in Brazil Amid Pandemic
articles.description: Brazil has dismantled several large, sophisticated counterfeit cash rings in recent months, indicating that criminal groups with the technological savvy to print fake banknotes are seizing on the economic crisis created by the coronavirus pandemic.

articles.title: Labor Initiatives in Women’s Prisons Struggle to Reduce Recidivism
articles.description: Labor initiatives inside women´s prisons in Latin America aim to improve employment opportunities for female inmates, so as to reduce the rate of repeat offenses. However, beyond prison walls, they face difficulties in securing job opportunities for former inmates.

articles.title: Middleman Linked Colombian Gangs with Israeli, Japanese Mafia
articles.description: An Israeli national suspected of ties to Japanese and Israeli mafia groups has been accused of running drug trafficking, money laundering, and sex trafficking operations in Colombia, revealing the logistical role of foreign brokers in organized crime in the Andean nation.

articles.title: Why Courtroom Corruption Has Costa Rica Concerned
